- Also called **eye space** or **camera space**. The origin is placed at the camera's lens.
- The View Matrix $V$ translates and rotates world coordinates so that the camera appears at the origin looking down the negative Z-axis.
- This space is essential for **frustum culling** — entities outside the view frustum are culled before rasterization, saving GPU cycles.

**3. Screen Space (Pixel Coordinates)**
- The final 2D pixel grid that appears on the player's monitor.
- Defined by viewport dimensions $(W, H)$ in pixels, with origin at the top-left and Y-axis pointing down.
- The Projection Matrix $P$ maps view-space coordinates into **Normalized Device Coordinates (NDC)** $x, y \in [-1, 1]$, which the viewport transform then maps to pixel coordinates.

**Transformation Chain:**
$$\vec{P}_{\text{screen}} = \text{Viewport}(P \cdot V \cdot M \cdot \vec{P}_{\text{local}})$$

This is the **MVP (Model-View-Projection) chain**.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Local Space\nMesh Vertices"] -->|Model Matrix M| W["World Space\nGlobal Scene Coords"]
    W -->|View Matrix V| V["View Space\nCamera Relative Coords"]
    V -->|Projection Matrix P| C["Clip Space\nNDC -1 to 1"]
    C -->|Viewport Transform| S["Screen Space\nPixel Coordinates"]
```

**(b)** With the help of suitable derivations, explain how the **perspective projection formula** $x' = \frac{f \cdot x}{z}$ is obtained from the pinhole camera model. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Pinhole Camera Setup.**
Place an ideal pinhole at the origin $O$ of a Cartesian coordinate system. The image plane is positioned at distance $f$ (focal length) along the positive Z-axis. A 3D world point $P(x, y, z)$ lies somewhere on the positive Z-axis side.

**Step 2 — Geometry of the Similar Triangles.**
The light ray from $P$ passes through the pinhole $O$ and strikes the image plane at point $P'(x', y', f)$. Two similar triangles are formed:
- Triangle 1: vertex at $P$, base $x$ on Z-axis, height $z$.
- Triangle 2: vertex at $P'$, base $x'$ on image plane, height $f$.

**Step 3 — Proportionality.**
Because these triangles are similar, the ratio of corresponding sides is equal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{x'}{f} &= \frac{x}{z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for $x'$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= \frac{f \cdot x}{z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Symmetry for the Y-axis.**
The same geometric reasoning on the vertical plane yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y' &= \frac{f \cdot y}{z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation.**
- When $z \to \infty$, $x' \to 0$, confirming that infinitely distant objects collapse to a single vanishing point.
- When $z \to f$, $x' \to x$, confirming that objects on the image plane appear at their true size.
- The non-linear $1/z$ division creates the **foreshortening** effect that gives 3D rendering its sense of depth.

This derivation is the geometric basis for the GPU's **perspective divide** in the rasterization stage.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **2D, 2.5D, and 3D game worlds** across at least six engineering parameters (storage, physics, AI, rendering, complexity, hardware). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 2D | 2.5D | 3D |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Coordinate Axes** | X, Y | X, Y (Z simulated) | X, Y, Z |
| **Vertex Storage** | 2 floats per vertex | 2 floats + Z-bucket | 3 floats per vertex |
| **Physics Engine** | Box2D, Chipmunk | Custom 2D + fake gravity | PhysX, Havok, Bullet |
| **AI Pathfinding** | 2D A*, NavMesh | 2D A* + heightmap | 3D NavMesh, HPA* |
| **Rendering API** | Sprite/Tilemap | Ray-cast, Mode 7, Billboard | OpenGL, Vulkan, D3D12 |
| **Collision Detection** | AABB, SAT (2D) | AABB + sector ID | BVH, OBB, GJK |
| **Hardware Demand** | Low (smartphone-safe) | Low–Moderate | High (GPU-dependent) |
| **Game-Design Complexity** | Simple | Medium | High |
| **Development Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Typical Genres** | Platformer, Arcade | Retro FPS, Racing | AAA FPS, RPG |

**Conclusion:** 2.5D is essentially a **performance optimization** trick — it preserves the rendering budget of 2D while faking the visual immersion of 3D, historically vital on hardware-limited consoles (SNES, PS1, early PCs).

**(b)** Derive the **2D Rotation Matrix** $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$ from first principles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Polar Representation.**
Consider a point $P(x, y)$ at distance $r$ from the origin and initial angle $\alpha$ from the positive X-axis:
$$x = r\cos\alpha, \quad y = r\sin\alpha$$

**Step 2 — Apply Rotation by Angle $\theta$.**
After rotating counter-clockwise by $\theta$, the new angle is $\alpha + \theta$, and the radius remains $r$:
$$x' = r\cos(\alpha + \theta), \quad y' = r\sin(\alpha + \theta)$$

**Step 3 — Expand Using Sum Identities.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= r(\cos\alpha\cos\theta - \sin\alpha\sin\theta) \\
y' &= r(\sin\alpha\cos\theta + \cos\alpha\sin\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $x$ and $y$ Back.**
Since $x = r\cos\alpha$ and $y = r\sin\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x' &= x\cos\theta - y\sin\theta \\
y' &= x\sin\theta + y\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Matrix Form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence the 2D rotation matrix is:
$$R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$$

**Verification:** Setting $\theta = 0$ gives the identity matrix (no rotation). Setting $\theta = \pi/2$ gives $R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, which correctly maps $(1, 0) \to (0, 1)$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting Units in the Formula Table:** Always state "FOV in degrees" or "FOV in radians" explicitly. Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for ambiguous unit assumptions.
> 2. **Confusing 2.5D with 3D:** Writing that 2.5D has "three dimensions" will cost full credit on the comparison question. 2.5D is **logically 2D**, only **visually** 3D.
> 3. **Skipping the Derivation Steps:** "Show the derivation" demands at least 4–5 explicit algebraic transitions. Writing only the final matrix is penalized heavily.
> 4. **Wrong Handedness:** Mixing OpenGL (right-handed, Z-out) with DirectX (left-handed, Z-in) conventions in the same answer confuses the examiner. Pick one and stick to it.
> 5. **Missing the "Time" Interpretation:** When asked about 4D worlds, students often describe only the academic spatial 4D and forget the **game-design 4th dimension = time**, which is the more commonly tested interpretation.
> 6. **No Block Diagram:** Even in a text-heavy answer, a labeled ASCII or Mermaid diagram is worth 1–2 marks in Part B (a) sub-parts. Never omit it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Dimension of a game world = number of spatial axes (DOF)** of translation available to entities. Mathematical representation is the vector space $\mathbb{R}^n$.
- ✅ **0D → 4D progression** maps roughly to the historical evolution of video games: text adventures → endless runners → arcade platformers → retro FPS → modern AAA → time-travel puzzlers.
- ✅ **2.5D is NOT 3D.** It is a 2D world with a faked perspective projection (ray-casting, Mode 7, parallax, billboards).
- ✅ **Three reference spaces** — World, View, Screen — are transformed by the **MVP matrix chain** $P \cdot V \cdot M$.
- ✅ **Perspective projection** $x' = \frac{fx}{z}$ comes from the **pinhole camera** model via similar triangles.
- ✅ **2D rotation matrix** is derived from the **polar form** plus the **angle-addition identities**: $R(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}$.
- ✅ **4D in games usually means TIME**, not a fourth spatial axis (except in rare 4D-puzzle titles).
- ✅ **Handedness matters:** Right-handed (OpenGL, Vulkan) vs Left-handed (DirectX, Unreal). Mixing them is a guaranteed bug source.
- ✅ **Storage cost scales linearly** with dimension count: $n$ floats per vertex. Memory budgeting must account for this.
- ✅ **View Frustum** is parameterized by **FOV, Aspect Ratio, Near Plane, Far Plane** — these four values define what the camera can see.
- ✅ **FOV in formulas uses radians**, but most engines expose it to designers in degrees. Convert via $\theta_{\text{rad}} = \theta_{\text{deg}} \cdot \pi / 180$.
- ✅ **The dot product** $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} = \vert\vec{a}\vert \vert\vec{b}\vert \cos\phi$ is the standard tool for "in-front-of / behind" visibility tests in 3D space.
- ✅ **Always include a block diagram** in 14-mark Part B sub-parts — it is a near-mandatory scoring element under KTU 2024 valuation rubrics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Realism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT745 - GAME DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Realism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Realism** in game development is the *engineering and artistic discipline* of emulating the sensory, physical, and behavioral characteristics of the real world within an interactive virtual environment. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, realism is classified into **five orthogonal pillars**:

1. **Visual Realism** — Photometric accuracy of geometry, lighting, and materials.
2. **Physics Realism** — Newtonian-accurate simulation of rigid bodies, fluids, and particles.
3. **Audio Realism** — Spatial propagation and environmental acoustic modeling.
4. **Animation Realism** — Biomechanically faithful skeletal motion and secondary dynamics.
5. **Behavioral Realism** — Cognitive and emergent fidelity of Non-Player Characters (NPCs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Realism* is the **degree of correspondence** between a simulated phenomenon and its empirical counterpart, measured across the **Visual–Physics–Audio–Animation–Behavior (VP²AB)** axis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Movie Set vs. The Theme Park**
> - A **movie set (low realism)** looks perfect from one camera angle — like a classic arcade game that *appears* 3D but has no real depth. You can't walk around it.
> - A **theme park ride (high realism)** uses real water, wind, and motion. The player exists *inside* the world — like a VR flight simulator where every photon, gust, and bullet is computed from first principles.
> - Game realism sits on a **spectrum**: from stylized (e.g., *Celeste*) to photoreal (e.g., *Unreal Engine 5 Lumen demo*). The art is choosing **where on the spectrum the fun lies**.

### 1.3 The Realism-Fun Trade-off Constant

A foundational KTU concept is the empirical observation that **100 % realism ≠ 100 % fun**. Real-world physics often produce *boring* outcomes (cars slide, bullets wobble). Hence, the **Game Design Triangle**:

$$
\text{Realism} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Fun} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Performance}
$$

Only **two of the three** can be maximized at any time. This is the **Game Designer's Trilemma** — a constant constraint in shipping a commercial title.

> [!TIP]
> The KTU examiner expects you to mention this **trade-off** whenever realism is discussed in a 14-mark question. It is a high-yield, frequently-tested concept.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Realism — Operational Breakdown

#### Pillar 1 — Visual Realism

Visual realism is governed by the **Rendering Equation** (Kajiya, 1986), which the KTU syllabus treats as the *master equation* of computer graphics:

$$
L_o(p,\omega_o) \;=\; L_e(p,\omega_o) \;+\; \int_{\Omega} f_r(p,\omega_i,\omega_o)\, L_i(p,\omega_i)\, (\omega_i \cdot \mathbf{n})\, d\omega_i
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $L_o(p,\omega_o)$ | Outgoing radiance from point $p$ in direction $\omega_o$ | $\mathrm{W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot sr^{-1}}$ |
| $L_e(p,\omega_o)$ | Emitted radiance (light source) | $\mathrm{W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot sr^{-1}}$ |
| $f_r(p,\omega_i,\omega_o)$ | Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function (**BRDF**) | $\mathrm{sr^{-1}}$ |
| $L_i(p,\omega_i)$ | Incoming radiance from direction $\omega_i$ | $\mathrm{W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot sr^{-1}}$ |
| $\omega_i \cdot \mathbf{n}$ | Cosine of angle between incident ray and surface normal | dimensionless |
| $\Omega$ | Hemisphere of incident directions | $\mathrm{sr}$ |

The **BRDF** $f_r$ is split by KTU into three physically-motivated lobes:

$$
f_r \;=\; f_{r,\text{diffuse}} \;+\; f_{r,\text{specular}} \;+\; f_{r,\text{transmission}}
$$

#### Pillar 2 — Physics Realism

Game physics engines (PhysX, Havok, Bullet, Box2D) solve **Newton-Euler** equations per substep at a fixed timestep, typically $\Delta t = \mathbf{1/60\;s}$:

$$
\mathbf{F} \;=\; m\,\mathbf{a} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \mathbf{v}_{t+\Delta t} \;=\; \mathbf{v}_t \;+\; \frac{\mathbf{F}}{m}\,\Delta t
$$

$$
\mathbf{x}_{t+\Delta t} \;=\; \mathbf{x}_t \;+\; \mathbf{v}_{t+\Delta t}\,\Delta t
$$

For rotation, the analogous **angular** form is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} \;=\; \mathbf{I}\,\boldsymbol{\alpha} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \boldsymbol{\omega}_{t+\Delta t} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\omega}_t \;+\; \mathbf{I}^{-1}\,\boldsymbol{\tau}\,\Delta t
$$

**Collision response** uses the **coefficient of restitution** $e \in [0,1]$:

$$
e \;=\; -\frac{v_{1,n}' - v_{2,n}'}{v_{1,n} - v_{2,n}}
$$

- $e = 0 \Rightarrow$ **perfectly inelastic** (objects stick).
- $e = 1 \Rightarrow$ **perfectly elastic** (kinetic energy conserved).

#### Pillar 3 — Audio Realism

Spatial audio uses the **Head-Related Transfer Function (HRTF)** to localize a sound source:

$$
H_{\text{HRTF}}(f,\theta,\phi) \;=\; \frac{Y_{\text{ear}}(f,\theta,\phi)}{Y_{\text{free}}(f,\theta,\phi)}
$$

Where $\theta$ is azimuth, $\phi$ is elevation, and $f$ is frequency. The left/right ears receive **filtered and time-delayed** versions of the source, which the auditory cortex decodes as 3D position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Realism requires **occlusion** (muffled sound through walls) and **reverb** (early reflections + late reverb tail). The standard model is **EAX / Steam Audio / Wwise** with ray-traced sound propagation.

#### Pillar 4 — Animation Realism

Skeletal animation uses **skinned meshes** with weighted bone transformations:

$$
\mathbf{v}_{\text{world}} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i(\mathbf{u}) \cdot \mathbf{T}_i \cdot \mathbf{B}_i(\mathbf{u})
$$

Where $w_i(\mathbf{u})$ is the blend weight at vertex $\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{T}_i$ is the bone transform, and $\mathbf{B}_i(\mathbf{u})$ is the bone-space vertex position. **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** solves for joint angles given an end-effector target — the **FABRIK** (Forward And Backward Reaching IK) algorithm is the KTU-recommended method.

#### Pillar 5 — Behavioral Realism

NPC behavior uses **Finite State Machines (FSM)**, **Behavior Trees (BT)**, or **Utility AI**:

$$
\text{Action}^* \;=\; \arg\max_{a \in \mathcal{A}} \; U(a, s, c)
$$

Where $U$ is a utility function, $s$ is world state, and $c$ is character context. Modern AAA titles use **GOAP** (Goal-Oriented Action Planning) and **HTN** (Hierarchical Task Network) planners.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Domain | Formula | Purpose / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Rendering | $L_o = L_e + \int_\Omega f_r L_i (\omega_i\cdot n) d\omega_i$ | Master rendering equation |
| 2 | Shading | $I = I_a k_a + \sum_i I_i (k_d(\mathbf{N}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{L}) + k_s(\mathbf{R}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{V})^n)$ | Phong illumination model |
| 3 | Shading (Blinn-Phong) | $I = I_a k_a + k_d I (\mathbf{N}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{L}) + k_s I (\mathbf{N}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{H})^n$ | Faster specular highlight |
| 4 | PBR | $f_r = \frac{F(\omega_o,h)\, G(n,\omega_i,\omega_o)\, D(h)}{4 (\omega_i\!\cdot\!n)(\omega_o\!\cdot\!n)}$ | Cook-Torrance microfacet BRDF |
| 5 | Physics | $\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}$ | Linear dynamics |
| 6 | Physics | $e = -(v_1' - v_2')/(v_1 - v_2)$ | Restitution (bounciness) |
| 7 | Physics | $\mathbf{x}_{t+\Delta t} = \mathbf{x}_t + \mathbf{v}\Delta t + \tfrac{1}{2}\mathbf{a}\Delta t^2$ | Verlet integration |
| 8 | Projectile | $R = \frac{v_0^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g}$ | Range of projectile |
| 9 | Projectile | $H = \frac{v_0^2 \sin^2\theta}{2g}$ | Maximum height |
| 10 | Audio | $H_{\text{HRTF}} = Y_{\text{ear}}/Y_{\text{free}}$ | Spatial localization |
| 11 | Audio | $I = I_0 / r^2$ | Inverse-square attenuation |
| 12 | LOD | $\text{Switch}(L) = \min\{k \mid d(\text{camera}, \text{object}) \ge D_k\}$ | Discrete level-of-detail |
| 13 | AI | $a^* = \arg\max_a U(a,s,c)$ | Utility AI selection |
| 14 | Animation | $\mathbf{v}_w = \sum_i w_i \mathbf{T}_i \mathbf{B}_i(\mathbf{u})$ | Linear blend skinning |
| 15 | Inverse Kinematics | Forward pass + Backward pass (FABRIK) | Reach a target joint |

> [!NOTE]
> The standard gravitational constant in KTU game-physics problems is **g = 9.81 m/s²**. The standard sound speed at sea level is **c = 343 m/s**.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility

Realism in games directly drives **simulation engineering** in adjacent industries:

- **Defense & Training:** Flight simulators (DCS, X-Plane) use high-fidelity physics for pilot certification.
- **Medical:** Surgical simulators (e.g., FundamentalVR) train surgeons with haptic realism.
- **Automotive:** Driving simulators (BeamNG, rFactor) test ADAS systems.
- **Architecture:** Real-time ray-traced previsualization (Unreal, Twinmotion).
- **Film:** Virtual production stages (LED volumes in *The Mandalorian*) demand photoreal real-time engines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Phong Reflection to Blinn-Phong

The classical **Phong shading equation** (1975) for a single point light is:

$$
I \;=\; I_a k_a \;+\; I_d \left[ k_d (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L}) \;+\; k_s (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V})^{\alpha} \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the expensive term.** Computing $\mathbf{R}$ requires a per-fragment reflection vector:

$$
\mathbf{R} \;=\; 2 (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L}) \mathbf{N} \;-\; \mathbf{L}
$$

This involves a normalized reflection and a dot product — costly when the view vector $\mathbf{V}$ changes per fragment.

**Step 2 — Introduce the half-vector.** Blinn (1977) proposed using the **halfway vector** $\mathbf{H}$ between $\mathbf{L}$ and $\mathbf{V}$:

$$
\mathbf{H} \;=\; \frac{\mathbf{L} + \mathbf{V}}{\lVert \mathbf{L} + \mathbf{V} \rVert}
$$

**Step 3 — Show equivalence in the limit.** When $\mathbf{N}$, $\mathbf{L}$, $\mathbf{V}$ are all unit length, and when the surface is "near-mirror," $\mathbf{H}$ approximates $\mathbf{N}$ at the highlight peak. Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{H} \;\approx\; \mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Rewrite the specular term:**

$$
k_s (\mathbf{R} \cdot \mathbf{V})^{\alpha} \;\approx\; k_s (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{H})^{\alpha}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Blinn-Phong equation:**

$$
I \;=\; I_a k_a \;+\; I_d \left[ k_d (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{L}) \;+\; k_s (\mathbf{N} \cdot \mathbf{H})^{\alpha} \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Performance gain.** The half-vector is computed **per-vertex** (or in a geometry shader) and interpolated, reducing per-fragment cost. This is why Blinn-Phong is the KTU default for real-time engines.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Coefficient of Restitution from Energy Conservation

**Given:** Two colliding rigid bodies of masses $m_1$, $m_2$ with pre-collision velocities $v_1$, $v_2$ and post-collision velocities $v_1'$, $v_2'$ along the collision normal.

**Step 1 — Conservation of Linear Momentum:**

$$
m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 \;=\; m_1 v_1' + m_2 v_2'
$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Coefficient of Restitution:**

$$
e \;=\; \frac{v_2' - v_1'}{v_1 - v_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the linear system.** From Step 2:

$$
v_1' \;=\; v_2' - e(v_1 - v_2)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Step 1:**

$$
m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 \;=\; m_1 [v_2' - e(v_1 - v_2)] + m_2 v_2'
$$

**Step 5 — Collect $v_2'$ terms:**

$$
m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 \;=\; (m_1 + m_2)\, v_2' \;-\; m_1 e (v_1 - v_2)
$$

**Step 6 — Isolate $v_2'$:**

$$
v_2' \;=\; \frac{m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 + m_1 e (v_1 - v_2)}{m_1 + m_2}
$$

**Step 7 — Symmetrically derive $v_1'$:**

$$
v_1' \;=\; \frac{m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 - m_2 e (v_1 - v_2)}{m_1 + m_2}
$$

**Step 8 — Special case check:** For $e = 0$ (perfectly inelastic), both velocities converge to the center-of-mass velocity:

$$
v_{\text{com}} \;=\; \frac{m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2}{m_1 + m_2}
$$

Substituting $e = 0$ into Steps 6 and 7 gives $v_1' = v_2' = v_{\text{com}}$. ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU exam frequently asks: *"A ball of mass 2 kg moving at 5 m/s strikes a stationary 3 kg ball. Find velocities after collision if $e = 0.8$."* — Use the formulas above.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Realism Technique

| Goal | Recommended Technique | Cost | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Real-time global illumination | **Screen-space GI (SSGI)** | Medium | Open worlds |
| Path-traced realism | **Monte Carlo Path Tracing** | Very High | Cutscenes, RTX demos |
| Convincing reflections | **SSR / RT reflections** | Medium–High | Puddles, mirrors |
| Soft shadows | **PCF / VSM** | Low | Outdoor scenes |
| Crowd behavior | **Flocking (Reynolds Boids)** | Low | Birds, enemies |
| Pathfinding | **A\* + NavMesh** | Low | Ground NPCs |
| Fluid simulation | **SPH / FLIP / Eulerian** | High | Water, blood |
| Cloth | **Mass-Spring / PBD** | Medium | Capes, garments |
| Destruction | **Voronoi fracture + RB** | High | Building collapse |
| Facial animation | **FACS + blendshapes** | Medium | Cutscenes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
