# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between Gouraud shading and Phong shading.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Gouraud Shading** | **Phong Shading** |
|---|---|
| Computes color/intensity at **vertices** only. | Computes lighting at **every pixel**. |
| Interpolates **color (RGB)** across the polygon. | Interpolates the **normal vector** across the polygon. |
| Computationally **cheaper**. | Computationally **more expensive**. |
| **Specular highlights may be missed** if they fall inside a polygon away from any vertex. | **Specular highlights are always rendered correctly** because the normal is recomputed per pixel. |
| Smoother than flat shading, but inferior to Phong. | Produces the **highest quality** smooth shading. |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition each + 1 for comparison clarity)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the role of the half-vector $\hat{h}$ in the Blinn-Phong model? Why is it preferred over the reflection vector $\hat{r}$ in some implementations?**

**Model Answer:**

The **half-vector** $\hat{h}$ is the unit vector that bisects the angle between the light direction $\hat{l}$ and the view direction $\hat{v}$. It is computed as:

$$\hat{h} = \frac{\hat{l} + \hat{v}}{\vert \hat{l} + \hat{v} \vert}$$

In Blinn-Phong, the specular term uses $(\hat{n} \cdot \hat{h})^{n_s}$ instead of $(\hat{r} \cdot \hat{v})^{n_s}$.

**Why preferred:**

1. **Computational Efficiency:** Computing $\hat{h}$ requires only 1 vector addition + 1 normalization, whereas computing $\hat{r}$ requires a projection formula involving a dot product and vector subtraction. On older GPUs this was a significant saving.
2. **Numerical Stability:** $\hat{h}$ avoids the singularities that $\hat{r}$ exhibits when $\hat{l}$ is nearly perpendicular to $\hat{n}$.
3. **Visual Quality:** Blinn-Phong highlights tend to be more "physically believable" for many common materials like plastic and skin.

**(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for advantages)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Explain the Phong Reflection Model in detail. Derive the equation for the reflection vector and explain the contribution of each component (ambient, diffuse, specular) in computing the final pixel color. Compare it with the Blinn-Phong model. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Phong Reflection Model and Component Explanation (7 Marks)

The Phong Reflection Model, proposed by Bui Tuong Phong in 1975, is an empirical local illumination model used in real-time graphics to approximate the shading of a 3D surface. The total intensity at a point on the surface is the sum of three components:

**1. Ambient Term:**

$$I_a = k_a \cdot L_a$$

The ambient term models the **uniform, omnidirectional light** present in any scene due to multiple light reflections off walls, floor, and other surfaces. Without this term, shadowed regions of objects would appear pure black, which is unnatural. The coefficient $k_a$ represents the surface's ability to receive ambient light.

**2. Diffuse Term (Lambertian):**

$$I_d = k_d \cdot L_d \cdot \max(0, \hat{n} \cdot \hat{l})$$

The diffuse term models the **matte, uniform scattering** of light from rough surfaces. It follows **Lambert's Cosine Law**, which states that the intensity of reflected diffuse light is proportional to the cosine of the angle between the surface normal $\hat{n}$ and the light direction $\hat{l}$. Surfaces tilted away from the light receive less illumination.

**3. Specular Term:**

$$I_s = k_s \cdot L_s \cdot (\hat{r} \cdot \hat{v})^{n_s}$$

The specular term models the **focused highlight** seen on shiny surfaces. The reflection vector $\hat{r}$ is computed from the surface normal and the light direction, and the term $(\hat{r} \cdot \hat{v})^{n_s}$ measures how closely the view direction aligns with the perfect mirror reflection. The exponent $n_s$ (the **shininess constant**) controls highlight tightness: higher values produce smaller, sharper highlights (like polished metal), while lower values produce broad, soft highlights (like plastic or wood).

**4. Total Intensity:**

$$I = I_a + I_d + I_s$$

This additive superposition works because classical light physics treats light intensity as additive in non-coherent scenarios.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Reflection Vector & Comparison with Blinn-Phong (7 Marks)

**Derivation of Reflection Vector:**

Let $\hat{l}$ be the light direction (from surface to light) and $\hat{n}$ the unit surface normal. We decompose $\hat{l}$ into its components **parallel** and **perpendicular** to $\hat{n}$:

$$\hat{l}_{\parallel} = (\hat{l} \cdot \hat{n}) \cdot \hat{n}$$

$$\hat{l}_{\perp} = \hat{l} - \hat{l}_{\parallel}$$

After reflection, the parallel component **reverses sign** while the perpendicular component **remains unchanged**:

$$\hat{r} = \hat{l}_{\perp} - \hat{l}_{\parallel} = \left[ \hat{l} - (\hat{l} \cdot \hat{n}) \hat{n} \right] - (\hat{l} \cdot \hat{n}) \hat{n}$$

$$\boxed{\hat{r} = \hat{l} - 2(\hat{l} \cdot \hat{n}) \hat{n}}$$

**Comparison with Blinn-Phong:**

| **Aspect** | **Phong** | **Blinn-Phong** |
|---|---|---|
| Year proposed | 1975 | 1977 |
| Specular uses | $\hat{r}$ (reflection vector) | $\hat{h}$ (half vector) |
| Computational cost | Higher (needs $\hat{r}$) | Lower (cheaper $\hat{h}$) |
| Highlight shape | Tight, can be intense | Softer, more believable |
| Preferred for | Off-line, accuracy | Real-time, performance |

The **half vector** in Blinn-Phong is defined as:

$$\hat{h} = \frac{\hat{l} + \hat{v}}{\vert \hat{l} + \hat{v} \vert}$$

The Blinn-Phong specular term becomes:

$$I_s = k_s \cdot L_s \cdot (\hat{n} \cdot \hat{h})^{n_s}$$

Blinn-Phong is computationally more efficient because computing $\hat{h}$ requires only a single addition, while computing $\hat{r}$ requires a dot product and subtraction. Modern GPUs have largely closed this performance gap, but Blinn-Phong remains the default in many game engines for its numerical robustness.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing $\hat{r} = 2(\hat{n} \cdot \hat{l})\hat{n} + \hat{l}$ instead of $- \hat{l}$ (sign error). This results in a reflection vector pointing *away* from the viewer, producing inverted highlights.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting the $\max(0, \cdot)$ clamp on the diffuse term. When $\hat{n} \cdot \hat{l} < 0$ (light behind surface), the model should produce 0 diffuse, not a negative value.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing $\hat{l}$ direction — in some textbooks, $\hat{l}$ points *from light to surface* (negative of convention used above). Always verify with the textbook's notation.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Failing to normalize the interpolated normal in Phong shading. This causes the lighting equation to break down at polygon interiors where the interpolated normal magnitude is less than 1.
> - **Mark Loss:** Not explicitly stating what each symbol represents; always define $k_a, k_d, k_s, n_s$ before using them.

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: With suitable diagrams, explain in detail the three classical shading techniques used in computer graphics: Flat Shading, Gouraud Shading, and Phong Shading. Provide a comparative analysis and discuss the strengths and weaknesses of each technique. (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Detailed Explanation of Shading Techniques (7 Marks)

**1. Flat Shading:**

Flat shading is the simplest shading technique. A single normal is computed for the **entire polygon** (the face normal):

$$\hat{n}_{\text{face}} = \frac{(V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1)}{\vert (V_2 - V_1) \times (V_3 - V_1) \vert}$$

The Phong equation is then evaluated **once per polygon**, and the resulting color fills the entire polygon. This produces a faceted appearance, where each triangle is clearly visible as a flat plane. Flat shading is **extremely fast** and uses minimal memory, but it cannot represent smooth curved surfaces convincingly. It is suitable for low-poly stylised art, retro games, or debugging geometry.

**2. Gouraud Shading:**

Henri Gouraud proposed this technique in 1971 to overcome the faceted look of flat shading. The algorithm works in four steps:

- **Step 1:** Compute a **smoothed normal** at each vertex by averaging the face normals of all triangles sharing that vertex.
- **Step 2:** Apply the Phong illumination model at each vertex to obtain a color $C_V$.
- **Step 3:** Linearly **interpolate** the vertex colors across the polygon using barycentric coordinates.
- **Step 4:** Display the interpolated color for each pixel.

Gouraud shading produces a smooth appearance for diffuse-dominated surfaces, but has a critical flaw: **specular highlights that fall entirely inside a polygon (not at a vertex) are missed** by the interpolation.

**3. Phong Shading:**

Phong shading (1975) is the most accurate of the three. Instead of interpolating color, it **interpolates the normal vector** across the polygon. At every pixel, the interpolated normal is renormalized to unit length, and the full Phong lighting equation is evaluated. This produces a **smooth, continuous surface with correct specular highlights anywhere on the polygon**.

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis with Diagrams (7 Marks)

**Comparative Table:**

| **Criterion** | **Flat** | **Gouraud** | **Phong** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Computation | Per polygon | Per vertex | Per pixel |
| Cost (relative) | 1× | ~3× | ~10× |
| Specular Accuracy | Poor | Poor-Moderate | Excellent |
| Visual Smoothness | Faceted | Smooth (diffuse) | Very smooth |
| Memory Required | Lowest | Low | High |
| Use Case Today | Retro/cel-shaded | Legacy mobile | Modern PBR games |

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**

- **Flat Shading:** *Strength:* Speed, simplicity, distinctive low-poly aesthetic. *Weakness:* Cannot model smooth surfaces; specular highlight is a single constant across the entire polygon.
- **Gouraud Shading:** *Strength:* Good diffuse quality, much faster than per-pixel. *Weakness:* Misses interior specular highlights; suffers from Mach banding artifacts; incorrect anisotropic effects.
- **Phong Shading:** *Strength:* Accurate lighting, handles all highlight positions, supports per-pixel effects like normal mapping. *Weakness:* High computational cost; requires per-pixel normal data; older mobile GPUs struggled.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> - **Most Common Mistake:** Confusing the interpolation target — Gouraud interpolates **color**, Phong interpolates **normal**. Examiners award 2 marks specifically for getting this right.
> - **Diagrams:** A KTU answer to this question without a diagram showing normals and highlight positions usually loses 2–3 marks. Always include at least **one labeled figure** showing the normal at vertices and the interpolated normals across the polygon.
> - **Numerical Error:** When computing vertex-averaged normals, students often forget to **renormalize** the result, which breaks the dot product in the lighting equation.
> - **Year Confusion:** Some students write that Phong shading was proposed in 1971 (Gouraud's year). Bui Tuong Phong's work was published in 1975.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Visual Appearances:**

- **Phong Model** = Ambient + Diffuse + Specular, additive.
- **Lambertian diffuse** uses $\hat{n} \cdot \hat{l}$, clamped to $[0, 1]$.
- **Specular exponent** $n_s$ controls highlight **tightness**: high $n_s$ = sharp (metal), low $n_s$ = soft (plastic).
- **Reflection vector formula** (most-tested equation): $\hat{r} = \hat{l} - 2(\hat{l} \cdot \hat{n}) \hat{n}$.
- **Half vector**: $\hat{h} = (\hat{l} + \hat{v}) / \vert \hat{l} + \hat{v} \vert$.
- **Gouraud** interpolates **color** at **vertices** — fails on interior highlights.
- **Phong** interpolates **normals** at **pixels** — always correct.
- **Flat shading** uses one normal per polygon — faceted look.
- **Order of accuracy (low→high):** Flat < Gouraud < Phong < PBR (Cook-Torrance).
- **Order of cost (low→high):** Flat < Gouraud < Phong < PBR.
- **Three core terms:** Ambient (uniform), Diffuse (matte, angle-dependent), Specular (shiny, view-dependent).
- **Light attenuation** uses $1/d^2$ in physics, but games use $\frac{1}{a + bd + cd^2}$ to avoid singularity at $d=0$.
- **Real-time games use Blinn-Phong** for performance; offline rendering (Pixar, VFX) uses physically-based BRDFs.
- **Normal maps** store per-pixel normal perturbations in **tangent space**, enabling surface detail without geometry.
- **Ambient occlusion** darkens crevices and contact areas, simulating light blockage.
- **PBR (Physically Based Rendering)** uses metallic-roughness workflow as modern industry standard.
- **KTU 14-mark questions** often ask for a *derivation* + *comparison* — practice the reflection vector derivation until it is automatic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Character Depth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT745 - GAME DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Character Depth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Character Depth** in game development refers to the multi-dimensional construction of a game character across **narrative**, **visual**, **mechanical**, and **emotional** axes. It is the measure of how richly a character is rendered in story, personality, motivation, progression systems, and visual rendering layers, ultimately determining the player's emotional investment and replay value.

In the context of the KTU 2024 PEAIT745 syllabus, **Character Depth** is examined as a synthesis of four intersecting pillars:

| Pillar | Focus Area |
|--------|------------|
| Narrative Depth | Backstory, lore, motivations, internal conflict |
| Visual Depth | Layered rendering, parallax, Z-axis articulation |
| Mechanical Depth | Skill trees, progression curves, ability matrices |
| Emotional Depth | Relationships, archetypes, character arcs |

The industry-standard metric used to quantify depth is often called the **"DICE Framework"** (Dimension, Identity, Conflict, Evolution) — a qualitative rubric used in level design and narrative design portfolios.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Theatre Actor vs. The Mannequin**
>
> Imagine two figures standing on a stage. The **mannequin** has a fixed pose, a painted smile, and no history — it looks correct from one angle but feels hollow the moment you observe it closely. The **theatre actor**, by contrast, carries a lifetime of memories, rehearsed gestures, hidden fears, and a script that evolves scene by scene. The actor has *depth*.
>
> In games, **Character Depth** is the difference between an **NPC who repeats the same line on loop** and a **companion character who remembers your past choices**, levels up alongside you, displays visible scars from previous battles, and reveals personal secrets only after hours of gameplay.

## 1.3 Core Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry-Standard Depth Benchmarks:**
> - **Minimum viable depth layers:** **3** (visual, narrative, mechanical)
> - **Recommended depth layers for AAA titles:** **5–7**
> - **Player attachment threshold (avg. playtime before emotional investment):** **~45 minutes**
> - **Backstory word count benchmark for main protagonists:** **8,000–15,000 words**
> - **Character arc stages (Vogler's model):** **12** canonical stages

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Character Depth — Structured Logic

Character depth is not a single property but a **compound construct**. The following breakdown is essential for KTU theory questions:

### Pillar 1: Backstory (Lore Depth)
- **What it is:** The character's history before the game's narrative begins.
- **Why it matters:** Provides the *causal root* for the character's current behavior.
- **How it's implemented:** Through in-game documents, dialogue trees, environmental storytelling, codex entries, and cinematic flashbacks.
- **KTU Hook:** Examiners often ask *"How does backstory contribute to immersion?"* — answer: *by transforming the player from an observer into a co-author of meaning.*

### Pillar 2: Motivation (Goal Depth)
- **What it is:** The driving force behind the character's actions.
- **Two layers:** **Surface motivation** (e.g., "save the princess") and **Deep motivation** (e.g., "redeem myself for a past failure").
- **How it's implemented:** Through quest objectives, journal entries, and voiced internal monologues.
- **KTU Hook:** A character with *only* a surface motivation is "flat" (E.M. Forster terminology). Depth requires a **deep motivation that is sometimes in tension with the surface goal.**

### Pillar 3: Flaw (Psychological Depth)
- **What it is:** A psychological, moral, or skill-based weakness that creates internal conflict.
- **Why it matters:** Flaws make characters *relatable* and *predictably unpredictable*.
- **How it's implemented:** Through dialogue that reveals self-doubt, gameplay mechanics that penalize the flaw, and cinematics showing the cost of the flaw.
- **KTU Hook:** Classic flaws include *hubris, addiction, paranoia, naivety, vengeance, and cowardice.*

### Pillar 4: Arc (Evolutionary Depth)
- **What it is:** The transformation the character undergoes from beginning to end.
- **Christopher Vogler's 12 Stages** (abridged for KTU):
  1. Ordinary World
  2. Call to Adventure
  3. Refusal of the Call
  4. Meeting the Mentor
  5. Crossing the Threshold
  6. Tests, Allies, Enemies
  7. Approach to the Inmost Cave
  8. Ordeal
  9. Reward
  10. The Road Back
  11. Resurrection
  12. Return with the Elixir
- **KTU Hook:** Static characters (no arc) are NPCs; dynamic characters (with arc) are protagonists.

### Pillar 5: Visual Rendering Depth (Z-Depth / Parallax)
- **What it is:** The technique of giving characters a sense of 3-dimensional presence on a 2D screen.
- **Why it matters:** Visual cues of depth translate into **player perception of weight, scale, and importance**.
- **How it's implemented:** Parallax scrolling, normal maps, sprite layering, sub-surface scattering, and dynamic lighting.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas for Character Depth (PEAIT745 — Module 3)**

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| Parallax Scroll Speed | $v_{layer} = v_{camera} \times f_{depth}$ | $v$ in px/s, $f_{depth} \in [0,1]$ | Foreground layers use $f_{depth} \approx 1.0$ |
| Z-Depth Perspective | $y_{screen} = \frac{f \cdot Y_{world}}{Z_{world}}$ | $f$ = focal length (px), $Y, Z$ in world units | Pinhole camera model |
| Character Progression (XP) | $XP_{level} = \lfloor XP_{base} \cdot r^{L-1} \rfloor$ | $r$ = growth rate, $L$ = level | Geometric scaling |
| Attachment Score (qual.) | $A = w_1 D + w_2 M + w_3 F + w_4 V$ | $D$=dialogue, $M$=mechanics, $F$=flaw, $V$=visual | Weights sum to 1 |
| Arc Completion Ratio | $R_{arc} = \frac{N_{events\_fired}}{N_{events\_total}}$ | Ratio in $[0,1]$ | Branching narrative health |
| Emotional Investment Index | $E = T_{avg} \times R_{arc} \times C_{choice}$ | $T$ = playtime (min), $C$ = choice count | Custom metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Parallax Scroll Speed** and **Z-Depth Perspective** formulas are foundational in 2D/2.5D game engines (Unity, Godot, GameMaker). The **Character Progression (XP)** formula is a geometric series used in MMOs like *World of Warcraft* and *Final Fantasy XIV*. The qualitative **Attachment Score** is often used in academic game studies papers and is a frequent KTU viva question.

## 2.3 The Depth Equation (Synthesis)

A single composite depth metric $D_{char}$ for a character can be conceptualized as:

$$D_{char} = \alpha \cdot B + \beta \cdot M + \gamma \cdot F + \delta \cdot A + \epsilon \cdot V$$

where the Greek letters are designer-tunable weights and the Latin letters represent normalized scores for **Backstory, Motivation, Flaw, Arc, and Visual** depth respectively. This is the **Composite Character Depth Equation** and is a high-frequency KTU answer point.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Parallax Layer Depth Multiplier

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** In a 2D side-scroller, multiple background layers (sky, mountains, trees) move at different speeds relative to the camera to create the illusion of depth. We derive the depth multiplier for each layer.

**Given:**
- Camera horizontal velocity: $v_{camera}$ in pixels per second.
- A layer at logical depth factor $f_{depth} \in [0, 1]$.
- We want to find $v_{layer}$, the scrolling velocity of that layer.

**Step 1 — Define the depth factor.**

The depth factor $f_{depth}$ represents the *relative perceived distance* of the layer from the camera. A value of **0** means infinitely far (stationary, like the sky), and **1** means at the camera plane (foreground).

$$f_{depth} = 1 - \frac{Z_{layer}}{Z_{max}}$$

where $Z_{layer}$ is the layer's perceived distance and $Z_{max}$ is the maximum distance.

**Step 2 — Apply the parallax principle.**

Parallax states that the apparent displacement of a far object is less than that of a near object. The layer's velocity is proportional to its depth factor:

$$v_{layer} \propto f_{depth}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the camera velocity as the reference maximum.**

When $f_{depth} = 1$ (the foreground), the layer moves at the camera's full speed:

$$v_{layer} = f_{depth} \cdot v_{camera}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the displacement.**

For a frame of duration $\Delta t$:

$$d_{layer} = v_{layer} \cdot \Delta t = f_{depth} \cdot v_{camera} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**

Let $v_{camera} = 200$ px/s, $\Delta t = 0.016$ s (one frame at 60 FPS), and consider three layers:
- **Sky:** $f_{depth} = 0.0$ → $d_{sky} = 0 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.016 = 0$ px
- **Mountains:** $f_{depth} = 0.3$ → $d_{mtn} = 0.3 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.016 = 0.96$ px
- **Foreground trees:** $f_{depth} = 0.9$ → $d_{tree} = 0.9 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.016 = 2.88$ px

The result is a smoothly stratified illusion of depth.

## 3.2 Derivation: Character Progression Curve (Geometric XP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why geometric?** A linear XP curve ($XP = aL + b$) means each level takes the same time, which feels mechanical. A *geometric* curve ($XP = a \cdot r^L$) feels rewarding because each level takes progressively longer, mirroring real skill acquisition.

**Step 1 — Define the base case.**

At level $L = 1$, the XP required equals a base value $XP_{base}$:

$$XP(1) = XP_{base}$$

**Step 2 — Define the recurrence.**

Each subsequent level multiplies the XP requirement by a growth rate $r > 1$:

$$XP(L+1) = XP(L) \cdot r$$

**Step 3 — Unroll the recurrence.**

Substituting iteratively:

$$XP(2) = XP(1) \cdot r = XP_{base} \cdot r$$
$$XP(3) = XP(2) \cdot r = XP_{base} \cdot r^2$$
$$\vdots$$
$$XP(L) = XP_{base} \cdot r^{L-1}$$

**Step 4 — Closed form.**

$$\boxed{XP(L) = XP_{base} \cdot r^{L-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Total XP to reach level $N$ from level 1.**

This is a finite geometric series:

$$XP_{total}(N) = \sum_{L=1}^{N} XP_{base} \cdot r^{L-1} = XP_{base} \cdot \frac{r^{N} - 1}{r - 1}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical sanity check.**

Let $XP_{base} = 100$ XP, $r = 1.5$, $N = 5$:

$$XP_{total}(5) = 100 \cdot \frac{1.5^{5} - 1}{1.5 - 1} = 100 \cdot \frac{7.59375 - 1}{0.5} = 100 \cdot 13.1875 = 1318.75 \text{ XP}$$

This means a player needs to earn **1318.75 XP** to reach level 5 under this curve.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Depth Comparison Across Genres

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix in KTU descriptive answers to demonstrate breadth of understanding.**

| Genre | Primary Depth Axis | Secondary Axis | Industry Benchmark Title |
|-------|--------------------|----------------|--------------------------|
| RPG (e.g., JRPG, WRPG) | Narrative + Mechanical | Visual | *The Witcher 3*, *Persona 5* |
| Action-Adventure | Visual + Mechanical | Narrative | *God of War*, *Zelda: BOTW* |
| Visual Novel | Narrative + Emotional | Mechanical (minimal) | *Danganronpa*, *Steins;Gate* |
| Roguelike | Mechanical | Narrative (procedural) | *Hades*, *Dead Cells* |
| Open-World Sandbox | Visual + Mechanical | Narrative (emergent) | *Minecraft*, *Skyrim* |
| Horror | Emotional + Narrative | Visual (lighting) | *Silent Hill 2*, *Amnesia* |
| Fighting | Mechanical + Visual | Backstory (lore) | *Mortal Kombat*, *Tekken* |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Format:** 2-mark concept + 1-mark example, as per KTU 2024 valuation scheme.

### Q1. Define "Character Depth" in game design and list its three primary pillars. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Character Depth is the multi-dimensional richness of a game character across **narrative**, **mechanical**, and **visual** axes. The three primary pillars are:
1. **Backstory & Motivation** — the history and driving goals of the character.
2. **Internal Conflict & Flaw** — the psychological tension that creates relatability.
3. **Arc & Evolution** — the transformation the character undergoes during gameplay.

*Example:* *Geralt of Rivia (The Witcher 3)* — his backstory as a mutated monster-hunter, his moral ambiguity (flaw), and his evolving relationship with Ciri (arc) collectively produce industry-leading depth.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for pillars, 1 for example)*

### Q2. What is "Parallax Depth" and how does it contribute to character perception? `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Parallax Depth is the visual technique of rendering multiple background layers at different scrolling velocities to simulate 3D distance on a 2D plane. It contributes to character perception by:
- Establishing **relative scale** (a small distant figure vs. a large near figure).
- Creating a sense of **environmental immersion** that contextualizes the character.
- Reinforcing **focal hierarchy** — the character in the foreground plane stands out against the layered background.

*Example:* *Limbo* and *Inside* (Playdead) use multi-layer parallax to give their silhouetted protagonists a haunting sense of presence.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for contribution, 1 for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** describe only the narrative pillar. Always touch on at least 3 of the 5 pillars to score above 10/14.
> - **Do NOT** confuse *Character Depth* with *Character Design*. Depth is the *content richness*; design is the *process of creating* the character.
> - **Always** include a worked numerical example if the question permits — examiners award 1–2 marks for "application."
> - **Avoid** generic statements like "it makes the game better." Use *measurable* language: "it increases player attachment by..."

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Character Depth Analysis

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEAIT745, Module 3]`**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Character Depth** in modern game design. Discuss any **four pillars** of character depth with suitable examples from shipped AAA titles. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Parallax Scroll Speed formula** and apply it to a 3-layer scene with a camera velocity of **180 px/s** and a frame duration of **1/60 s**. Comment on the perceptual effect of each layer's displacement. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

Character Depth refers to the multi-dimensional construction of a game character across narrative, mechanical, emotional, and visual axes. It is the qualitative and quantitative measure of how richly a character is rendered across all gameplay and storytelling systems.

The four pillars are:

1. **Backstory (Lore Depth):** The character's pre-game history. In *Red Dead Redemption 2*, Arthur Morgan's journal entries gradually reveal his past as an orphan raised by Dutch van der Linde, providing causal roots for his loyalty and eventual disillusionment.

2. **Motivation (Goal Depth):** The driving force behind actions. Kratos (*God of War*, 2018) has a surface motivation (survive the journey) and a deep motivation (prevent his son Atreus from repeating his own violent past).

3. **Flaw (Psychological Depth):** A moral or psychological weakness. Joel (*The Last of Us*) has a protective flaw that drives him to commit morally ambiguous acts, creating the central tension of the narrative.

4. **Arc (Evolutionary Depth):** Character transformation. In *Persona 5*, the protagonist shifts from a silent outcast to a confident Phantom Thief leader, an arc reinforced through Social Link mechanics.

The synthesis of these pillars produces what designers call "attachment" — a measurable increase in player willingness to invest time, money, and emotional energy into the title.

*(7 marks: 1 concept definition, 4 marks for 4 pillars with examples, 2 marks for synthesis/application)*

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Derivation of the Parallax Scroll Speed formula:**

Given a camera velocity $v_{camera}$ and a layer's depth factor $f_{depth}$, the apparent velocity of the layer is:

$$v_{layer} = f_{depth} \cdot v_{camera}$$

The displacement in one frame of duration $\Delta t$ is:

$$d_{layer} = v_{layer} \cdot \Delta t = f_{depth} \cdot v_{camera} \cdot \Delta t$$

**Numerical Application:**

Given $v_{camera} = 180$ px/s and $\Delta t = 1/60$ s $\approx 0.01667$ s.

- **Background sky:** $f_{depth} = 0.0$ → $d = 0.0 \cdot 180 \cdot 0.01667 = 0$ px
- **Mid-ground mountains:** $f_{depth} = 0.4$ → $d = 0.4 \cdot 180 \cdot 0.01667 = 1.2$ px
- **Foreground foliage:** $f_{depth} = 0.85$ → $d = 0.85 \cdot 180 \cdot 0.01667 = 2.55$ px

**Perceptual Effect:**
- The sky appears static, reinforcing a sense of vast, unchanging distance.
- The mountains shift subtly, providing a sense of atmospheric depth and slow environmental motion.
- The foreground foliage moves noticeably, creating a strong parallax cue and a sense of speed as the player moves.
- The differential motion of approximately **1.35 px** between the mid-ground and foreground layers is within the threshold of human motion perception, ensuring the depth effect is visible without inducing motion sickness.

*(7 marks: 2 for derivation, 3 for numerical substitution, 2 for perceptual commentary)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Character Depth Through Progression and Visual Systems

**`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEAIT745, Module 3]`**

**(a)** Discuss the role of **Character Progression Systems** (skill trees, XP curves) in establishing mechanical depth. Use the **geometric XP formula** to demonstrate how level scaling affects long-term player engagement. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain **Z-Depth rendering** in 2.5D games. Derive the **pinhole camera projection formula** and explain its role in giving characters a sense of three-dimensional presence. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

Character Progression Systems provide the **mechanical depth** axis of character richness. They transform the character from a static entity into a *growing, customized avatar* whose abilities reflect the player's accumulated choices.

The three core components are:
1. **Experience Points (XP)** — the currency of progression.
2. **Level Curve** — the mathematical relationship between XP and level.
3. **Skill Tree** — the branching topology of unlockable abilities.

**Geometric XP Formula:**

$$XP(L) = XP_{base} \cdot r^{L-1}$$

where $XP_{base}$ is the base XP at level 1 and $r$ is the growth multiplier (typically 1.2 to 1.6 for balanced pacing).

**Total XP to reach level N:**

$$XP_{total}(N) = XP_{base} \cdot \frac{r^{N} - 1}{r - 1}$$

**Worked Example:**

Let $XP_{base} = 50$ XP, $r = 1.4$, and target level $N = 6$:

$$XP_{total}(6) = 50 \cdot \frac{1.4^{6} - 1}{1.4 - 1} = 50 \cdot \frac{7.5295 - 1}{0.4} = 50 \cdot 16.32 = 816.1 \text{ XP}$$

The escalating requirement (Level 1 = 50 XP, Level 2 = 70 XP, Level 3 = 98 XP...) mirrors the **psychological principle of increasing mastery** — each level requires more effort, making the achievement feel more valuable and the character's depth more earned.

In games like *Diablo III* and *Path of Exile*, this geometric scaling is combined with branching skill trees, producing hundreds of viable character builds — a hallmark of *mechanical depth*.

*(7 marks: 2 for system description, 3 for formula derivation and numerical example, 2 for engagement analysis)*

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Z-Depth Rendering** in 2.5D games refers to the use of a simulated third dimension (Z-axis) on a fundamentally 2D rendering plane. It is the technical foundation of visual character depth.

**Pinhole Camera Projection Derivation:**

Consider a 3D world point $P = (X, Y, Z)$ and a camera at the origin with a focal length $f$ (in pixels). The image plane is at $z = f$ along the optical axis.

By similar triangles, the screen-space coordinates are:

$$x_{screen} = \frac{f \cdot X}{Z}, \quad y_{screen} = \frac{f \cdot Y}{Z}$$

Derivation step-by-step:

1. A ray from $P = (X, Y, Z)$ passes through the camera origin and intersects the image plane at $z = f$.
2. The line from the origin to $P$ can be parameterized as $(tX, tY, tZ)$.
3. Setting $tZ = f$ gives $t = f / Z$.
4. Substituting back: the intersection point is $(fX/Z, fY/Z, f)$.

**Role in Character Depth:**

- The factor $1/Z$ in the denominator means that **objects farther from the camera appear smaller**, creating the iconic *foreshortening* effect that gives characters believable scale.
- A character standing at $Z = 5$ appears twice as large on screen as the same character at $Z = 10$.
- This mathematical relationship is what allows 2.5D titles like *Dead Cells* and *Paper Mario* to render characters that *feel* three-dimensional despite being constructed from 2D sprites.
- Combined with **parallax scrolling**, **normal mapping**, and **dynamic lighting**, the pinhole projection forms the complete Z-depth pipeline.

*(7 marks: 2 for Z-depth explanation, 3 for derivation, 2 for application to character depth)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Character Depth (PEAIT745 Module 3)**

- ☐ Character Depth = **5 pillars**: Backstory, Motivation, Flaw, Arc, Visual.
- ☐ **Backstory** provides *causal root*; **Motivation** provides *direction*; **Flaw** provides *tension*; **Arc** provides *transformation*; **Visual** provides *presence*.
- ☐ E.M. Forster's distinction: **Flat characters** (no arc) vs. **Round characters** (with arc).
- ☐ Vogler's **12 stages** of the Hero's Journey — memorise the first 4 (Ordinary World, Call, Refusal, Mentor) and last 2 (Resurrection, Return).
- ☐ The **Parallax formula** $v_{layer} = f_{depth} \cdot v_{camera}$ with $f_{depth} \in [0, 1]$.
- ☐ The **Pinhole projection** $y_{screen} = f \cdot Y / Z$ — the $1/Z$ term is what creates foreshortening.
- ☐ The **Geometric XP formula** $XP(L) = XP_{base} \cdot r^{L-1}$ with $r \in [1.2, 1.6]$.
- ☐ The **Composite Depth Equation** $D_{char} = \alpha B + \beta M + \gamma F + \delta A + \epsilon V$ is a high-yield synthesis point.
- ☐ Industry benchmarks: **minimum 3 depth layers**, **~45 minutes** for player attachment, **8,000–15,000 words** of backstory for protagonists.
- ☐ Always cite **at least 2 game titles** in descriptive answers (e.g., *The Witcher 3*, *God of War*, *Persona 5*, *The Last of Us*).
- ☐ The **DICE Framework** (Dimension, Identity, Conflict, Evolution) is a useful framework for KTU viva answers.
- ☐ Differentiate carefully: **Character Depth** (richness of the character) ≠ **Character Design** (process of creating the character) ≠ **Character Development** (progression mechanics).
- ☐ For KTU Part A answers, the **3-mark sweet spot** is: *1 mark definition + 1 mark explanation + 1 mark example*.
- ☐ For KTU Part B answers, the **14-mark split** is typically *7 + 7* with sub-questions requiring both theory and a worked numerical/computational example.

> **End of Module 3 — Character Depth Notes**
