<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nonlinear Stories (Nonlinear Narrative)**: A narrative design paradigm in interactive media (specifically video games) in which the sequence, outcome, and exposition of plot events are not fixed into a single chronological path. Instead, the story evolves through **player agency**, dynamic state changes, **branching decision nodes**, emergent gameplay events, and conditional triggers that collectively allow multiple valid paths, reordered chapters, divergent character arcs, and alternate endings to coexist within a single authored experience.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *Nonlinear Stories* is a sub-topic under **Module 3: Game Storytelling and Narrative Design**, and it sits at the intersection of *Game Design*, *Narrative Theory*, and *Software Architecture*. A nonlinear story is technically implemented as a **directed graph (or finite-state machine)** of narrative states, where each node represents a *story beat*, *scene*, or *plot point*, and each edge represents a *player-triggered transition* governed by conditions, flags, or accumulated variables.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Choose-Your-Own-Adventure Bookshelf"**
> Imagine you walk into a library, but instead of pulling one novel from the shelf, you are handed a *Choose-Your-Own-Adventure* book. The librarian whispers, *"Every decision you make rewrites the chapters that exist for you."* A **linear story** is like reading a single novel from page 1 to page 300 — fixed, predictable, and identical for every reader. A **nonlinear story** is like that adventure book: at the end of Chapter 5, you are asked *"Do you open the red door or the blue door?"* — your choice (1) determines the next chapter, (2) changes which characters remain alive, (3) may lock out entire plot arcs, and (4) ultimately leads to one of many possible endings. In games, you are not just *reading* the adventure — you are *playing* it, so the "doors" are replaced by dialogue choices, combat outcomes, exploration routes, and moral dilemmas.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Game-Design Vocabulary

- **Branching Factor (B)**: Average number of meaningful choices presented to the player at any given decision node. KTU textbooks typically cite B = 2 to 5 for *manageable complexity*.
- **Narrative Depth (D)**: Maximum number of sequential decision points the player can experience in a single playthrough.
- **Total Story States (N)**: $N \approx B^D$ — the exponential growth of unique story paths.
- **Player Agency Score (PAS)**: A qualitative measure (0–10) of how much the player meaningfully affects the story.
- **Illusion of Choice (IoC)**: A scenario where the player *believes* they are making a meaningful decision, but the eventual outcome is identical regardless of the choice.

### 1.4 Syllabus-Highlighted Sub-Topics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically identifies the following sub-concepts under "Nonlinear Stories":
> 1. **Branching Narrative Structures** — tree, web, and graph topologies.
> 2. **Player Choice and Consequence Systems** — flag-based, variable-based, and reputation-based.
> 3. **Multiple Endings** — good/bad/secret, faction-based, and "true" endings.
> 4. **Dynamic & Adaptive Storytelling** — emergent narratives and AI Director systems.
> 5. **Open-World & Sandbox Narratives** — location-driven and quest-hub storytelling.
> 6. **Dialogue Trees & Quest Branching** — the implementation micro-layer.
> 7. **Narrative Coherence Challenges** — authorial control vs player freedom.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Taxonomy of Nonlinear Stories

Nonlinear stories are not a monolith. KTU examiners expect students to recognize **four canonical structural archetypes**, each with distinct mathematical, narrative, and engineering properties.

### A. Branching Tree Structure
- The classic *Choose-Your-Own-Adventure* model.
- Each decision point forks the narrative into mutually exclusive sub-branches.
- Mathematically modeled as a **rooted tree** with finite depth.
- **Strengths**: Authorial control, clear consequences, easy to author.
- **Weaknesses**: Exponential state explosion ($N \approx B^D$), high production cost, players may miss 60–80% of content on a single playthrough.

### B. Web / Graph Structure
- Story nodes are connected in a non-hierarchical, web-like topology.
- The player can move *between* nodes in multiple valid orders.
- The narrative state is captured by a set of *world flags* (binary or scalar variables).
- **Strengths**: High replayability, supports "butterfly effect" designs, encourages exploration.
- **Weaknesses**: Risk of narrative incoherence, requires rigorous state management.

### C. Open-World / Sandbox Structure
- The story is *location-driven* rather than *plot-driven*.
- Quests and story beats are tied to geographic regions and can be triggered in any order.
- Often relies on **level-scaling** and **dynamic difficulty**.
- **Strengths**: Maximum player agency, emergent stories (e.g., *Skyrim*, *Breath of the Wild*).
- **Weaknesses**: Weak central narrative thrust, risk of "main plot disconnect".

### D. Dynamic / Adaptive Structure
- The narrative *responds in real time* to player behavior, often via an AI subsystem (e.g., Valve's **AI Director** in *Left 4 Dead*).
- Tension curves, enemy spawns, and even plot revelations are algorithmically modulated.
- **Strengths**: Personalized experience, high replayability.
- **Weaknesses**: Authorial intent is harder to enforce; often described as "systemic" rather than "narrative".

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Nonlinear Design

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use nonlinear stories?**
> - To **increase replayability** (re-engagement value).
> - To **deepen emotional investment** through player-driven consequence.
> - To **simulate moral complexity** (no single "correct" answer).
> - To **leverage the medium's unique advantage** — interactivity is wasted on linear plots.

> [!NOTE]
> **How are they built?**
> The canonical implementation pipeline is:
> 1. **Author a story graph** (nodes = scenes, edges = choices).
> 2. **Attach condition functions** to each edge (e.g., `if (playerHasItem("Key") && morality > 50)`).
> 3. **Maintain a global state dictionary** (flags, variables, reputation scores).
> 4. **Resolve the next active node** by evaluating all edges from the current node.
> 5. **Persist state** across save/load cycles.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total Story States | $N$ | $N \approx B^{D}$ | Predicts authoring workload |
| Effective Choice Rate | $E$ | $E = \frac{\text{meaningful\_decisions}}{\text{total\_decisions}}$ | Detects *Illusion of Choice* |
| Replayability Index | $R$ | $R = 1 - \frac{1}{N_{endings}}$ | Quantifies ending diversity |
| Path Convergence | $C$ | $C = \frac{\text{shared\_nodes}}{\text{total\_nodes}}$ | Measures graph density |
| Player Agency Score | $PAS$ | $PAS = \alpha \cdot E + \beta \cdot R + \gamma \cdot C$ | Weighted composite metric |
| Authoring Cost | $K$ | $K \propto N \cdot \text{cost\_per\_node}$ | Budget planning |
| State Space | $S$ | $S = 2^{f}$ where $f$ = number of binary flags | Memory/QA complexity |
| Mean Path Length | $\bar{L}$ | $\bar{L} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{P} L_i}{P}$ | Average playthrough duration |
| Flag Coherence | $\phi$ | $\phi = \frac{\text{reachable\_valid\_states}}{2^{f}}$ | Detects dead/unreachable states |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vertical Pipe Rule**: Notice the use of `\vert` in $E = \alpha \cdot E + \beta \cdot R + \gamma \cdot C$ and similar — never write `|x|` inside a markdown table to avoid syntax corruption.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Game Industry**: Core design pattern in RPGs, visual novels, and AAA action-adventures.
- **Simulation & Training**: Military, medical, and aviation simulators use branching narratives for scenario-based training.
- **Interactive Fiction & Education**: Twine, Ink, and ChoiceScript power educational "branching textbooks".
- **UX & Persuasive Design**: Branching onboarding flows (e.g., Duolingo, Khan Academy) borrow from nonlinear narrative theory.
- **AI Safety & Ethics**: Branching moral scenarios are used to test alignment in large language models and autonomous agents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Exponential Story-State Explosion

**Problem:** A game designer creates a nonlinear story with a branching factor of $B = 3$ and a narrative depth of $D = 8$ decision points. How many unique story states exist in the design document, and what is the production implication?

### Step-by-Step Logical Walk-Through

**Step 1 — Identify the structural model.**
We are modeling the story as a *k-ary tree* of depth $D$, where each node (decision point) has exactly $B$ children (possible next states).

**Step 2 — Apply the k-ary tree node-count formula.**
The number of leaf nodes (terminal story states) in a full $B$-ary tree of depth $D$ is given by:

$$
N_{leaves} = B^{D}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values.**
With $B = 3$ and $D = 8$:

$$
N_{leaves} = 3^{8}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the exponent.**

$$
3^{8} = (3^{2})^{4} = 9^{4} = 6561
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate total nodes (internal + leaf).**
The total number of nodes in the tree is the geometric series:

$$
N_{total} = \sum_{i=0}^{D} B^{i} = \frac{B^{D+1} - 1}{B - 1}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify.**

$$
N_{total} = \frac{3^{9} - 1}{3 - 1} = \frac{19683 - 1}{2} = \frac{19682}{2} = 9841
$$

**Step 7 — Production-cost interpretation.**
If each node costs an average of 5 developer-hours (scripting, voice acting, QA), the total cost is:

$$
K = 9841 \times 5 = 49205 \text{ developer-hours}
$$

**Step 8 — Conversion to person-years.**
Assuming 1 FTE = 1600 hours/year:

$$
\text{Person-years} = \frac{49205}{1600} \approx 30.75 \text{ FTE-years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** This is why production teams *deliberately converge* branches near the climax (e.g., the "Corridor of Convergence" pattern seen in *Mass Effect 2/3*) to keep $N$ tractable.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Player Agency Score (PAS) Composite

**Problem:** Compute the Player Agency Score for a narrative where the *Effective Choice Rate* $E = 0.85$, *Replayability Index* $R = 0.92$, and *Path Convergence* $C = 0.40$, with weights $\alpha = 0.5$, $\beta = 0.3$, $\gamma = 0.2$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Walk-Through

**Step 1 — State the PAS formula.**

$$
PAS = \alpha \cdot E + \beta \cdot R + \gamma \cdot C
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values.**

$$
PAS = (0.5)(0.85) + (0.3)(0.92) + (0.2)(0.40)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute each product individually.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.5)(0.85) &= 0.425 \\
(0.3)(0.92) &= 0.276 \\
(0.2)(0.40) &= 0.080
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the components.**

$$
PAS = 0.425 + 0.276 + 0.080 = 0.781
$$

**Step 5 — Scale to 0–10 (if the design rubric uses that scale).**

$$
PAS_{10} = 0.781 \times 10 = 7.81 \text{ / } 10
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A PAS of 7.81 indicates a *high-agency* design — typical of titles like *Detroit: Become Human* or *The Witcher 3*.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Linear vs Nonlinear Story Trade-Offs

| Design Axis | Linear Story | Branching Tree | Web/Graph | Open-World Sandbox |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Authorial Control** | Maximum | High | Medium | Low |
| **Player Agency** | Minimal | High | Very High | Maximum |
| **Production Cost** | Low | Exponential | High | High |
| **Replayability** | Single Playthrough | Moderate (2–4) | High (4–8) | Very High (10+) |
| **Narrative Coherence** | Guaranteed | Easy to manage | Requires flags | At risk |
| **Cinematic Immersion** | Excellent | Good | Variable | Lower |
| **Test Coverage Burden** | $O(D)$ | $O(B^D)$ | $O(B^D)$ | $O(2^f)$ |
| **Best Genres** | Action, FPS | Visual Novel, RPG | RPG, Adventure | Sandbox RPG, Survival |
| **Canonical Examples** | *Uncharted*, *Last of Us* | *Detroit: BH* | *Mass Effect* | *Skyrim*, *BotW* |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: State-Space Coherence Check

**Problem:** A game has $f = 12$ binary story flags. Compute the *theoretical* state space and the *reachable* state space if only 800 of the theoretical states are narratively valid.

### Step-by-Step Logical Walk-Through

**Step 1 — Compute the theoretical state space.**

$$
S_{theoretical} = 2^{f} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ states}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the reachable (valid) states.**
Given: $S_{valid} = 800$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Flag Coherence metric $\phi$.**

$$
\phi = \frac{S_{valid}}{S_{theoretical}} = \frac{800}{4096}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the fraction.**

$$
\phi = \frac{800}{4096} = \frac{25}{128} \approx 0.1953
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result.**
A $\phi$ of **19.53%** indicates that roughly 80% of the theoretical flag combinations represent *narratively impossible* states (e.g., "King is dead AND King just gave a speech"). This is healthy — it means the narrative is well-constrained and free of contradictions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram A — Linear vs Nonlinear Story Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[START] --> nodeB[Scene 1: Intro]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Scene 2: Inciting Incident]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Scene 3: Climax]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Scene 4: Resolution]
    nodeE --> nodeF[END: Single Outcome]

    classDef linear fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE,nodeF linear
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The above is a **linear story graph** — a single chain from START to END with exactly one outcome. No player choice alters the path.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram B — Branching Tree Nonlinear Story

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[Start: Village Burns] --> d1{Choice 1: Save Family or Pursue Raider}
    d1 --> p1a[Path A: Family Saved]
    d1 --> p1b[Path B: Raider Confronted]
    p1a --> d2{Choice 2: Trust Elder or Investigate Alone}
    d2 --> p2a[Path A1: Elder Questline]
    d2 --> p2b[Path A2: Solo Investigation]
    p1b --> d3{Choice 3: Spare or Execute Raider}
    d3 --> p3a[Path B1: Spared, Joins Party]
    d3 --> p3b[Path B2: Executed, Reputation Loss]
    p2a --> e1[Ending 1: Village Restored]
    p2b --> e2[Ending 2: Secret Conspiracy Revealed]
    p3a --> e3[Ending 3: Redemption Arc]
    p3b --> e4[Ending 4: Tyrant Ascendant]

    classDef choice fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef path fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000
    classDef ending fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class d1,d2,d3 choice
    class p1a,p1b,p2a,p2b,p3a,p3b path
    class e1,e2,e3,e4 ending
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Yellow diamonds are *decision nodes*; blue rectangles are *path segments*; green rectangles are *terminal endings*. This produces $N_{endings} = 4$ from a depth of $D = 3$ and $B = 2$ in the lower branches.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram C — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Nonlinear Narrative Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[INPUT LAYER]
        ip1[Player Input Devices]
        ip2[Dialogue UI]
        ip3[Quest Log UI]
    end

    subgraph CL[CONTROL LOGIC LAYER]
        c1[Event Bus]
        c2[Condition Evaluator]
        c3[Flag Manager]
        c4[Reputation Tracker]
        c5[Inventory Checker]
    end

    subgraph NL[NARRATIVE LAYER]
        n1[Story Graph Database]
        n2[Dialogue Tree Engine]
        n3[Quest Branch Resolver]
        n4[Cutscene Trigger Manager]
    end

    subgraph OL[OUTPUT LAYER]
        o1[Cinematic Playback]
        o2[NPC Behavior Update]
        o3[World State Mutation]
        o4[UI Notification]
    end

    ip1 --> c1
    ip2 --> c1
    ip3 --> c1
    c1 --> c2
    c2 --> c3
    c2 --> c4
    c2 --> c5
    c3 --> n1
    c4 --> n1
    c5 --> n1
    n1 --> n2
    n1 --> n3
    n1 --> n4
    n2 --> o1
    n3 --> o2
    n4 --> o3
    n2 --> o4

    classDef input fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000000
    classDef logic fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,color:#000000
    classDef narrative fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000000
    class ip1,ip2,ip3 input
    class c1,c2,c3,c4,c5 logic
    class n1,n2,n3,n4 narrative
    class o1,o2,o3,o4 output
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *Input Layer* captures player actions and routes them through an *Event Bus* to the *Control Logic Layer*, which evaluates story-graph conditions against a global flag/reputation/inventory state. The *Narrative Layer* resolves the next active node and dispatches commands to the *Output Layer* (cinematics, NPC behavior, world mutation, UI). This is the canonical architecture of nonlinear narrative engines such as *Ink*, *Yarn Spinner*, and *articy:draft*.

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram D — Convergence Pattern for Cost Control

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[Act 2 Opening] --> brA[Branch A: Diplomatic Path]
    root --> brB[Branch B: Stealth Path]
    root --> brC[Branch C: Combat Path]
    brA --> conv[Act 3 Convergence Point: Final Battle]
    brB --> conv
    brC --> conv
    conv --> fin1[Ending 1: Sacrifice]
    conv --> fin2[Ending 2: Victory]
    conv --> fin3[Ending 3: Twist]

    classDef branch fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,color:#000000
    classDef converge fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef ending fill:#D1FAE5,stroke:#047857,color:#000000
    class brA,brB,brC branch
    class conv,root converge
    class fin1,fin2,fin3 ending
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The **Corridor of Convergence** pattern — branches diverge in Act 2 but reconverge at a shared Act 3 node. This dramatically reduces authoring cost ($N$ goes from $B^D$ to $B^{D_1} \times E^{D_2}$ where $E$ is the converged branch count) while preserving player agency in the early game.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define "Nonlinear Story" in the context of game narrative design. Differentiate it from a linear narrative with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *nonlinear story* is a narrative structure in which the sequence, branching, and resolution of plot events are not fixed; instead, the player’s decisions, actions, or in-game conditions dynamically alter the story’s progression, leading to multiple valid paths or endings. In contrast, a *linear narrative* follows a single predetermined sequence of events identical for every player.
- **Example of Linear**: *Uncharted 4* — the story unfolds in a fixed cinematic sequence with no major branching.
- **Example of Nonlinear**: *Detroit: Become Human* — the player makes choices that lead to drastically different character fates and multiple endings.
*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for contrast, 1 mark for examples.)*

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **What is an "Illusion of Choice" in game narratives? Give one example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The *Illusion of Choice* (IoC) is a design technique in which the player is presented with a decision interface (dialogue options, moral choices, etc.) that *appears* meaningful but ultimately converges to the same narrative outcome regardless of the player’s selection.
- **Example**: In *The Last of Us*, the player can choose to spare or kill certain characters, but the main plot remains largely unaffected. Another classic example is the *Coleman in Mass Effect 2* recruitment (paragon/renegade flags that converge).
- **Purpose**: Used to preserve authorial narrative control while still granting the *psychological satisfaction* of agency.
*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for purpose.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1 (–2 marks):** Students often describe nonlinear stories *only* as "branching", ignoring web-graph, open-world, and dynamic/adaptive variants. Always mention *all four* structural archetypes.
> - **Pitfall 2 (–2 marks):** Failing to quantify with formulas ($B^D$, $2^f$, PAS) loses marks in the analytical sub-part.
> - **Pitfall 3 (–1 mark):** Not citing *named canonical examples* (e.g., *Mass Effect*, *Detroit: Become Human*, *Skyrim*) is a common deduction.
> - **Pitfall 4 (–1 mark):** Confusing *player agency* with *open world* — they are related but distinct (an open world can still have a strictly linear main quest, e.g., *Breath of the Wild*’s "Defeat Ganon" is linear even if order is free).

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four major structural archetypes of nonlinear stories in game design. For each, state (i) its topology, (ii) one canonical game example, and (iii) one key advantage and one key disadvantage.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A studio is designing a nonlinear RPG with branching factor $B = 4$ and narrative depth $D = 6$ decision points. The design team has decided to use a *Convergence Pattern* that reduces the effective terminal states from the theoretical maximum by 75%. Compute: (i) the theoretical number of leaf states, (ii) the effective number of leaf states after convergence, and (iii) the percentage reduction in authoring cost if the reduction in states is proportional to the cost reduction. Comment on the design implications.

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| # | Archetype | Topology | Example | Advantage | Disadvantage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Branching Tree** | Rooted tree | *Detroit: Become Human* | Clear cause-effect, strong authorial control | Exponential state explosion |
| 2 | **Web / Graph** | Cyclic graph | *Mass Effect 2* | High replayability, "butterfly effect" | Risk of narrative incoherence |
| 3 | **Open-World** | Geographic hub-and-spoke | *The Witcher 3* | Maximum player freedom | Weak central narrative thrust |
| 4 | **Dynamic/Adaptive** | AI-modulated flow | *Left 4 Dead* (AI Director) | Personalized pacing | Hard to enforce authorial intent |

*(Award 1.5 marks per archetype; balance across the four columns for full credit.)*

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the theoretical number of leaf states.**

$$
N_{leaves} = B^{D} = 4^{6}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the exponent.**

$$
4^{6} = (2^{2})^{6} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective leaf states after a 75% reduction.**

$$
N_{effective} = N_{leaves} \times (1 - 0.75) = 4096 \times 0.25 = 1024
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the cost-reduction percentage.**
Since the cost is proportional to the state count:

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{N_{leaves} - N_{effective}}{N_{leaves}} \times 100\% = \frac{4096 - 1024}{4096} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$
\text{Reduction} = \frac{3072}{4096} \times 100\% = 0.75 \times 100\% = 75\%
$$

**Step 6 — Comment on design implications.**
The convergence pattern reduces authoring cost by **75%**, freeing roughly **15,360 developer-hours** (assuming 5 hours/node) for reinvestment in voice acting, cinematics, or QA. The trade-off is a *less divergent* narrative — the player may perceive that "all roads lead to Rome", reducing the *Effective Choice Rate (E)* and hence the Player Agency Score. Designers must therefore ensure that the *qualitative differences* across the 1024 surviving paths remain meaningful (e.g., different companions, different final cutscenes, different epilogues) rather than purely cosmetic.

*(Award 2 marks each for steps 1–3, 1 mark for the percentage, 2 marks for the design comment.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the functional architecture of a *Nonlinear Narrative Engine*. Identify and explain the role of at least four core components, and show how a typical *flag-based consequence system* propagates from player input to world-state mutation.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A visual-novel game uses a state dictionary with $f = 10$ binary flags to track relationship outcomes with three characters (love, neutral, hostile). The narrative designer claims that 90% of the theoretical state space is narratively reachable. Compute: (i) the theoretical state space, (ii) the claimed reachable states, and (iii) the Flag Coherence metric $\phi$. Discuss whether the designer's claim is healthy for a coherent narrative, and what QA challenges arise when $\phi$ approaches 1.0.

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Block Diagram (refer to Mermaid Diagram C in Section 4.3):**
The architecture consists of **four layers**:

1. **Input Layer (1 mark):** Captures player actions — dialogue selections, quest log interactions, controller inputs. Routes them as events to the *Event Bus*.
2. **Control Logic Layer (2 marks):** The *Event Bus* dispatches events to the *Condition Evaluator*, which queries the *Flag Manager* (binary/scalar state), *Reputation Tracker* (faction scores), and *Inventory Checker*. For example, a player choosing *"I forgive you"* sets the flag `forgive_companion = true`.
3. **Narrative Layer (2 marks):** The *Story Graph Database* (nodes = scenes, edges = conditional transitions) is queried. The *Dialogue Tree Engine* and *Quest Branch Resolver* determine the next active node based on the new flag state. Example: the edge `companion_forgiven → companion_stays_with_party` is now traversable.
4. **Output Layer (1 mark):** The resolved node dispatches commands to *Cinematic Playback*, *NPC Behavior Update*, *World State Mutation* (e.g., spawn/despawn a follower), and *UI Notification* (e.g., "Companion rejoined the party").
5. **Flag-based consequence flow (1 mark):** Player input → Event Bus → Condition Evaluator → Flag update → Story Graph re-evaluation → World mutation → Player-visible output.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Compute the theoretical state space.**

$$
S_{theoretical} = 2^{f} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ states}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the claimed reachable states.**

$$
S_{reachable} = 0.90 \times 1024 = 921.6 \approx 922 \text{ states}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Flag Coherence metric $\phi$.**

$$
\phi = \frac{S_{reachable}}{S_{theoretical}} = \frac{922}{1024}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$
\phi = \frac{922}{1024} = \frac{461}{512} \approx 0.9004 \text{ (or 90.04\%)}
$$

**Step 5 — Discussion of the designer's claim (2 marks).**
A $\phi \approx 0.90$ is **dangerously high** for a coherent visual novel. It implies that *only 10%* of the theoretical flag combinations are *narratively impossible* (e.g., "love AND hostile with the same character"). This is a *red flag* — it suggests the author has not adequately constrained the story graph, and players may encounter contradictory states such as:
- A character who is simultaneously *in love* and *hostile*.
- A character who is *alive* (per one flag) but *confirmed dead* (per another).
- A quest objective that was both *completed* and *failed*.

**Step 6 — QA Challenges (2 marks).**
When $\phi \to 1.0$, the QA burden grows as $O(2^f)$ because virtually every combination must be tested for *internal consistency*, *dialogue consistency*, and *visual state* (character models, UI indicators). Best practice is to keep $\phi$ in the range **0.15–0.30**, which is what well-authored games like *Mass Effect* achieve. The designer should introduce *mutex constraints* (e.g., a character cannot be both `love_A` and `hate_A` simultaneously) to prune the state space.

*(Award 2 marks each for steps 1–4, 2 marks for the discussion, 1 mark for the QA challenge, 1 mark for the mutex recommendation.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Nonlinear Stories**

- **Definition**: A nonlinear story is a *player-driven* narrative whose sequence, branches, and endings vary based on in-game decisions and state conditions.
- **Four Archetypes**: (1) Branching Tree, (2) Web/Graph, (3) Open-World Sandbox, (4) Dynamic/Adaptive — *name all four in any long-answer question*.
- **Branching Factor $B$**: Average meaningful choices per decision node; typically $B = 2$ to $5$.
- **Narrative Depth $D$**: Number of sequential decision points in a single playthrough.
- **Total Story States**: $N \approx B^D$ — the *exponential explosion* formula.
- **Flag Coherence $\phi$**: $\phi = S_{valid} / 2^f$ — measures narrative consistency; healthy range is **15–30%**.
- **Player Agency Score (PAS)**: Composite metric combining $E$, $R$, $C$ with designer-chosen weights.
- **Illusion of Choice**: A *design tool*, not a flaw — it preserves authorial control while satisfying the player.
- **Convergence Pattern**: Used to cap $N$ by merging branches before the climax; reduces cost by up to **75%**.
- **State Management**: Flags (binary), variables (scalar), reputation (faction), and inventory are the *four primitive state types*.
- **Tools of the Trade**: *Ink* (by Inkle), *Yarn Spinner*, *articy:draft*, *Twine*, *ChatMapper* — know at least two.
- **Canonical Examples**:
  - *Branching Tree* → *Detroit: Become Human*, *Until Dawn*.
  - *Web/Graph* → *Mass Effect* series, *The Witcher 3*.
  - *Open-World* → *Skyrim*, *Breath of the Wild*.
  - *Dynamic/Adaptive* → *Left 4 Dead* (AI Director), *Shadow of Mordor* (Nemesis System).
- **Key Trade-off**: Player agency ⇄ Authorial control (more freedom ⇒ more QA cost ⇒ more inconsistency risk).
- **Production Heuristic**: If $B^D > 1000$, *strongly consider* a convergence pattern.
- **Exam Tip**: For any 14-mark question on nonlinear stories, *always* include (i) the four archetypes, (ii) at least one formula, (iii) a block diagram, and (iv) a named game example.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Topic:** Granularity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Granularity** in game development is the measure of *fineness*, *resolution*, or *level of detail* at which a game's simulation, rendering, or decision-making subsystem operates. It quantifies how finely a continuous or high-dimensional game world is discretized into perceivable, processable, and renderable units — across the **temporal**, **spatial**, **behavioural**, and **semantic** axes of game design.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (mapped to the NEP 2020 outcome-based framework), granularity is treated as a **first-class design parameter** that directly governs the trade-off triangle of **Fidelity ↔ Performance ↔ Cost**. The course outcome target for this module requires the student to:

- **CO₃ (Apply):** Select appropriate granularity levels for physics, AI, and rendering subsystems.
- **CO₄ (Analyze):** Evaluate the impact of granularity choices on frame-rate stability, simulation determinism, and player experience.

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Axes of Granularity

| Axis | Question it Answers | Typical Unit |
|------|---------------------|--------------|
| **Temporal Granularity** | *How often does the simulation tick?* | Hertz (**Hz**) or seconds (**s**) |
| **Spatial Granularity** | *How detailed is the world geometry?* | Metres (m), polygons, or texels |
| **Behavioural Granularity** | *How detailed is each agent's reasoning?* | Decision cycles per second |
| **Semantic Granularity** | *How detailed is the data model?* | Bytes per entity, class hierarchy depth |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU exams, **temporal** and **spatial** granularity carry **~70%** of the typical mark weight. Always define both before answering.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Microscope Zoom" Metaphor

Imagine you are filming a nature documentary:

- A **wide-angle lens** shows the entire forest — you see movement of herds but miss individual ants. This is **COARSE granularity**.
- A **macro lens** shows a single leaf — you see every vein and aphid but lose the herd. This is **FINE granularity**.
- A **documentary director** must constantly **switch lenses** as the story demands — sometimes you need the herd, sometimes the ant. This switching is exactly what a **Granularity Manager** does inside a game engine.

The director never films every frame at maximum magnification because the camera crew, film stock, and audience attention are all **finite resources** — exactly like a GPU, CPU, and player's perceptual bandwidth.

## 1.4 Why Granularity Matters in Modern Games

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Granularity is **never** "the finer the better." The KTU syllabus explicitly emphasises that inappropriate granularity causes:
> - **Over-granulation** → wasted CPU/GPU cycles, frame-time spikes, **tunneling** in collision.
> - **Under-granulation** → visual aliasing, jittery AI, missed collisions, **unrealistic physics**.

The art of game engineering lies in **adaptive granularity** — varying the level of detail dynamically based on distance, priority, and available budget.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Temporal Granularity — The Heartbeat of the Simulation

### 2.1.1 Fixed Timestep (Deterministic Granularity)

In a **fixed timestep** model, the game state is advanced by a constant Δt regardless of the actual wall-clock time between frames. Glenn Fiedler's *"Fix Your Timestep!"* formulation is the canonical reference adopted in KTU Module 3.

The accumulator pattern runs as follows:

$$
\Delta t_{\text{fixed}} = \frac{1}{N_{\text{simHz}}}
$$

where $N_{\text{simHz}}$ is the **simulation rate** (commonly **60 Hz**, **120 Hz**, or **240 Hz** for competitive games).

The number of sub-steps required per render frame is:

$$
N_{\text{sub}} = \left\lfloor \frac{\Delta t_{\text{frame}}}{\Delta t_{\text{fixed}}} \right\rfloor
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state that the **accumulator** prevents the *spiral of death* by capping $N_{\text{sub}}$ at a maximum (typically **5 to 8**).

### 2.1.2 Variable Timestep (Performance-Adaptive Granularity)

Here Δt is taken straight from the last frame's duration. Cheaper computationally but **non-deterministic** — replays and networked multiplayer become harder to keep in sync.

$$
\Delta t_{\text{var}} = t_{\text{now}} - t_{\text{prev}}
$$

### 2.1.3 Semi-Fixed (Hybrid) Timestep — Industry Standard

Used by Unreal Engine and Unity's *Time.fixedDeltaTime*. Physics uses $\Delta t_{\text{fixed}}$; rendering uses $\Delta t_{\text{var}}$ for interpolation.

## 2.2 Spatial Granularity — Level of Detail (LOD)

Spatial granularity partitions a 3D asset into multiple representations. The standard LOD distance function is:

$$
d_{\text{switch}}(i \rightarrow i+1) = d_{\text{max}} \cdot r^{i}
$$

where:
- $d_{\text{max}}$ is the distance at which LOD **0** switches to LOD **1**.
- $r$ is the **LOD bias ratio** (typically **0.5 ≤ r ≤ 0.7**).
- $i$ is the LOD index starting from 0.

The total memory saved when reducing from LOD $n$ to LOD $m$ is:

$$
S_{\text{mem}} = \sum_{i=m}^{n-1} \bigl( V_i - V_{i+1} \bigr)
$$

where $V_i$ is the vertex/polygon count at LOD level $i$.

## 2.3 Behavioural Granularity — AI Decision Cycles

AI agents make decisions on their own clock. The granularity ratio is:

$$
G_{\text{AI}} = \frac{f_{\text{decision}}}{f_{\text{render}}}
$$

- $G_{\text{AI}} = 1$ → think every render frame (**expensive**).
- $G_{\text{AI}} = 0.1$ → think every 10 frames (**efficient**, **responsive**).
- $G_{\text{AI}} = 0.01$ → think once per second (**strategy level**).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Glossary | Typical Range |
|---|----------------|-----------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\Delta t = 1 / f_{\text{sim}}$ | $\Delta t$ = step size, $f$ = frequency | 4.17 ms – 16.67 ms |
| 2 | $N_{\text{sub}} = \lfloor \Delta t_{\text{frame}} / \Delta t_{\text{fixed}} \rfloor$ | sub-step count | 1 – 8 |
| 3 | $f_{\text{Nyquist}} \geq 2 \cdot f_{\text{max event}}$ | sampling theorem bound | Hz |
| 4 | $d_{\text{LOD switch}} = d_0 \cdot r^{i}$ | LOD distance | m |
| 5 | $\text{Aliasing risk} \propto 1 / \Delta t$ | temporal aliasing | qualitative |
| 6 | $G_{\text{AI}} = f_{\text{decision}} / f_{\text{render}}$ | AI granularity ratio | 0 – 1 |
| 7 | $\text{Tunneling prob.} \propto v \cdot \Delta t / h_{\text{collider}}$ | velocity, step, collider height | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Poly budget} = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} V_i$ | total triangle budget | triangles |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Rule — Nyquist Sampling:** A simulation must run at **at least twice** the highest event frequency in the game (e.g., a 30 Hz physics tick cannot faithfully represent a 20 Hz bouncing ball without aliasing).

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Aerospace simulators** (Boeing 737 MAX, F-16) demand **fixed 120 Hz** granularity to prevent pilot-induced oscillations.
- **Mobile games** rely heavily on **dynamic LOD switching** to maintain 30 fps on low-end GPUs.
- **Cloud gaming (GeForce Now, Xbox Cloud)** uses **network-aware temporal granularity** to throttle simulation rate when bandwidth drops.
- **VR headsets (90 Hz minimum)** require **ultra-fine temporal granularity** to prevent motion sickness.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Safe Velocity to Prevent Tunneling

**Problem Setup:** A collider of thickness $h$ is sampled every $\Delta t$. An object moving at velocity $v$ must not "tunnel" through the collider in a single step.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Distance travelled in one step} &= v \cdot \Delta t \\
\text{Safety constraint: } v \cdot \Delta t &\le h \\
\therefore \; v_{\text{max}} &= \frac{h}{\Delta t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Textual interpretation:**
- **Line 1:** The displacement during a single physics tick equals velocity multiplied by the time step.
- **Line 2:** To prevent the object from skipping past the collider, this displacement must be less than or equal to the collider's thickness.
- **Line 3:** Solving for $v$ yields the maximum safe linear velocity.

**Numerical Example (typical exam question):**
Given $h = 0.5 \, \text{m}$ and $\Delta t = 16.67 \, \text{ms} = 0.01667 \, \text{s}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{max}} &= \frac{0.5}{0.01667} \\
v_{\text{max}} &\approx 30.0 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This corresponds to **108 km/h** — relevant for racing-game design.

## 3.2 Derivation: LOD Switch Distances (Geometric Progression)

Starting from the general formula $d_i = d_0 \cdot r^{i}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_0 &= d_0 \cdot r^{0} = d_0 \quad \text{(LOD 0 region: closest, finest)} \\
d_1 &= d_0 \cdot r^{1} \quad \text{(LOD 1 switches at } d_1 \text{)} \\
d_2 &= d_0 \cdot r^{2} \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
d_{n-1} &= d_0 \cdot r^{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked numerical example:** Suppose $d_0 = 50 \, \text{m}$ and $r = 0.6$. Compute the first four switch distances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_0 &= 50 \times 0.6^{0} = 50.0 \; \text{m} \\
d_1 &= 50 \times 0.6^{1} = 30.0 \; \text{m} \\
d_2 &= 50 \times 0.6^{2} = 18.0 \; \text{m} \\
d_3 &= 50 \times 0.6^{3} = 10.8 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Beyond 50 m the model is fully culled; from 30–50 m we use LOD 1; from 18–30 m LOD 2; and below 10.8 m the full LOD 3 mesh.

## 3.3 Derivation: AI Granularity Energy Budget

Let $E_{\text{decision}}$ be joules (or, equivalently, CPU cycles) consumed per AI think step, and $E_{\text{frame budget}}$ be the total per-frame energy available for AI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{agents}} \cdot E_{\text{decision}} \cdot f_{\text{decision}} &\le E_{\text{frame budget}} \\
\therefore \; f_{\text{decision,max}} &= \frac{E_{\text{frame budget}}}{N_{\text{agents}} \cdot E_{\text{decision}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** With $E_{\text{frame budget}} = 2.0 \, \text{ms}$ of CPU, $N_{\text{agents}} = 200$, $E_{\text{decision}} = 0.01 \, \text{ms}$ per agent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\text{decision,max}} &= \frac{2.0}{200 \times 0.01} \\
&= \frac{2.0}{2.0} = 1.0 \; \text{Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each AI agent may think **at most once per second** to stay within the 2 ms AI budget — a classic coarse-granularity decision in RTS games.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Fine Granularity | Coarse Granularity |
|----------|-----------------|--------------------|
| **CPU/GPU cost** | High | Low |
| **Visual fidelity** | High | Low (aliasing risk) |
| **Determinism** | Higher (smaller Δt) | Lower (larger Δt) |
| **Network sync burden** | Larger packet rate | Lower packet rate |
| **Tunneling probability** | Lower | Higher |
| **Best for** | VR, fighting, FPS | MMO, mobile, RTS |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Granularity Hierarchy in a Game Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Game Engine Master Clock] --> B[Render Granularity]
    A --> C[Physics Granularity]
    A --> D[AI Granularity]
    A --> E[Network Granularity]
    A --> F[Audio Granularity]

    B --> B1[Variable Delta t]
    B --> B2[Adaptive LOD]

    C --> C1[Fixed Delta t 60Hz]
    C --> C2[Solver Iterations]
    C --> C3[Sub-step Cap]

    D --> D1[Reactive Tick 10Hz]
    D --> D2[Deliberative Tick 1Hz]
    D --> D3[Group Decisions]

    E --> E1[Snapshot Rate 20Hz]
    E --> E2[Interest Mgmt]

    F --> F1[Sample Rate 44.1kHz]
    F --> F2[Voice Pool Limit]
```

## 4.2 Adaptive LOD State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> LOD0
    LOD0 --> LOD1 : d greater than d0 * r
    LOD1 --> LOD0 : d less than d0 * r
    LOD1 --> LOD2 : d greater than d0 * r squared
    LOD2 --> LOD1 : d less than d0 * r squared
    LOD2 --> LOD3 : d greater than d0 * r cubed
    LOD3 --> LOD2 : d less than d0 * r cubed
    LOD3 --> Culled : d greater than d0
    Culled --> LOD3 : d less than d0
```

## 4.3 Temporal Granularity Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Start of Frame] --> Q{Is Determinism Required?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Use Fixed Delta t]
    Q -- No --> S{Performance Critical?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Use Variable Delta t]
    S -- No --> U[Use Semi-Fixed Delta t]
    R --> V[Accumulator Pattern]
    V --> W[Run N sub Physics Steps]
    W --> X[Interpolate Render State]
    T --> X
    U --> X
    X --> Y[Present Frame]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define temporal and spatial granularity in game development. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Temporal granularity** is the fineness of the time step used to advance the game simulation. *Advantage:* better physics determinism and smoother animations. *Disadvantage:* higher CPU cost per second of gameplay.
>
> **Spatial granularity** is the fineness of geometric detail retained in the world model, typically expressed via Level of Detail (LOD). *Advantage:* efficient use of GPU and memory. *Disadvantage:* visual popping and aliasing when objects switch LODs abruptly.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the Nyquist criterion and why is it important when choosing physics simulation granularity?**

**Model Answer:**

The Nyquist criterion states that the sampling frequency must be **at least twice** the highest frequency component of the signal being sampled. In a game, if physics events occur at $f_{\text{max}}$, the simulation must run at $\geq 2 f_{\text{max}}$ to avoid **temporal aliasing** (e.g., a ball appearing to bounce backward or freeze mid-air). This is why physics engines use 60 Hz or 120 Hz fixed timesteps regardless of the render rate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** confuse *temporal* granularity with *spatial* granularity — examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for swapped definitions.
> - **Always** quote the numerical value of constants (e.g., **60 Hz**, **16.67 ms**) — vague answers lose marks.
> - **Diagrams carry weight** — a labelled LOD diagram or timestep flow earns **2 bonus marks** under the 2024 scheme.
> - **Units are mandatory** in derivations; forgetting units costs **1 mark** per occurrence.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain with neat diagrams the **fixed timestep** and **variable timestep** approaches to temporal granularity in game loops. Derive the sub-step count formula and state how the "spiral of death" is prevented. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A racing game uses a fixed physics step of $\Delta t = 16.67 \, \text{ms}$. The track contains a wall of thickness $h = 0.4 \, \text{m}$. Compute the maximum safe linear velocity of a car. If the same game is ported to mobile with a fixed step of $\Delta t = 33.33 \, \text{ms}$, by what percentage does the maximum safe velocity change? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Fixed vs Variable Timestep — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define both.

- **Fixed timestep:** simulation advances by a *constant* $\Delta t_{\text{fixed}}$ each tick.
- **Variable timestep:** simulation advances by the *actual elapsed wall-clock time* $\Delta t_{\text{frame}}$.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Diagram — present the **accumulator pattern** as in Section 4.3.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Derive the sub-step count:

$$
N_{\text{sub}} = \left\lfloor \frac{\Delta t_{\text{frame}}}{\Delta t_{\text{fixed}}} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Explain spiral-of-death prevention:

> If the rendering thread stalls, $\Delta t_{\text{frame}}$ grows, which would normally demand **more** physics sub-steps, which would consume **more** CPU, which would stall the render **further** — a positive feedback loop called the *spiral of death*. The accumulator breaks this loop by **capping** $N_{\text{sub}}$ at a constant (e.g., **$N_{\text{max}} = 5$**) and *dropping* the remaining time, accepting a small slowdown.

---

**(b) Maximum Safe Velocity — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State the safety condition:

$$
v_{\text{max}} \cdot \Delta t \le h
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Solve for the PC case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{max,PC}} &= \frac{h}{\Delta t} = \frac{0.4}{0.01667} \\
&\approx 24.0 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Solve for the mobile case:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{\text{max,mobile}} &= \frac{0.4}{0.03333} \\
&\approx 12.0 \, \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Compute the percentage change:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta v\,\% &= \frac{v_{\text{max,mobile}} - v_{\text{max,PC}}}{v_{\text{max,PC}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{12.0 - 24.0}{24.0} \times 100 \\
&= -50\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (extra credit):** On mobile, the safe velocity is **halved**. Designers must either reduce car top-speeds, increase wall thickness, or use **continuous (swept) collision detection** to compensate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, describe the **Level of Detail (LOD)** mechanism used to control spatial granularity. Derive the LOD switch distance formula $d_i = d_0 \cdot r^{i}$ and explain the role of the **LOD bias ratio** $r$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A game world uses three LOD levels for a tree model. The switch distances are $d_0 = 60 \, \text{m}$, $r = 0.5$. Compute the three switch distances, the vertex-count saved when switching from LOD 0 (8 000 verts) to LOD 1 (3 500 verts) to LOD 2 (1 200 verts), and the total memory saving in **percentage**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) LOD Mechanism — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** LOD is a spatial-granularity technique where the same object is represented by multiple mesh resolutions. The engine selects the appropriate mesh based on the camera distance.

**Step 2 (3 Marks):** Derivation of switch distance:

- Let $d_0$ be the **outer** distance at which LOD 0 stops being rendered.
- For a geometric progression with common ratio $r$, the $i$-th switch distance is $d_i = d_0 \cdot r^{i}$.
- $r \in [0.4, 0.8]$ is the **LOD bias**: $r \to 1$ means LOD changes happen far away (more fine detail retained); $r \to 0$ means LOD changes happen close (aggressive culling).

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Diagram — present the LOD switching flow as in Section 4.2.

---

**(b) Three LOD Distances and Memory Savings — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Compute the switch distances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_0 &= 60 \times 0.5^{0} = 60.0 \; \text{m} \\
d_1 &= 60 \times 0.5^{1} = 30.0 \; \text{m} \\
d_2 &= 60 \times 0.5^{2} = 15.0 \; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Vertex savings per transition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{LOD 0} \rightarrow \text{LOD 1} &: \Delta V_{0 \to 1} = 8000 - 3500 = 4500 \text{ verts} \\
\text{LOD 1} \rightarrow \text{LOD 2} &: \Delta V_{1 \to 2} = 3500 - 1200 = 2300 \text{ verts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Total saving in **percentage**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total saved} &= 4500 + 2300 = 6800 \text{ verts} \\
\text{Original} &= 8000 \text{ verts} \\
\text{Saving}\,\% &= \frac{6800}{8000} \times 100 = 85\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Conclusion — adopting LODs reduces the vertex load by **85%** in the worst case, freeing GPU budget for additional scene complexity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Granularity**

- 🎯 **Granularity = Level of Detail** of a game's simulation across **time**, **space**, **behaviour**, and **data**.
- ⏱️ **Temporal granularity** is governed by $\Delta t = 1 / f_{\text{sim}}$; common values are **60 Hz** (16.67 ms), **120 Hz** (8.33 ms).
- 🔁 **Fixed timestep** = deterministic + stable physics but **CPU-hungry**; uses the **accumulator pattern** with a **sub-step cap (5–8)** to avoid the *spiral of death*.
- 📈 **Variable timestep** = cheap but **non-deterministic**; avoided in networked/multiplayer contexts.
- 🧊 **Semi-fixed (hybrid)** = physics fixed, render interpolated (Unity / Unreal default).
- 🗺️ **Spatial granularity** is implemented via **Level of Detail (LOD)** with switch distances $d_i = d_0 \cdot r^{i}$, $r \in [0.4, 0.8]$.
- 🤖 **AI granularity ratio** $G_{\text{AI}} = f_{\text{decision}} / f_{\text{render}}$ decides how "smart" agents feel.
- ⚠️ **Nyquist criterion:** physics rate $\geq 2 \times$ highest event frequency to avoid aliasing.
- 🏎️ **Tunneling condition:** $v_{\text{max}} = h / \Delta t$; lower $\Delta t$ ⇒ higher safe velocity.
- 💾 **LOD memory savings:** $S_{\%} = (V_{\text{base}} - V_{\text{coarsest}}) / V_{\text{base}} \times 100$.
- 🧪 **VR requirement:** minimum **90 Hz** temporal granularity to prevent motion sickness.
- 🌐 **Networked games** favour **coarser** temporal granularity to reduce bandwidth.
- 📊 **Adaptive (dynamic) granularity** is always preferred over static — match fidelity to importance.
- 📝 **For KTU answers:** always pair the *type* of granularity with a *quantitative metric* and a *real game example* to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedback and Rewarding the Player: How the Player Gets Through and Continues Playing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT745 - GAME DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Feedback and Rewarding the Player: How the Player Gets Through and Continues Playing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Game Design** and **Player Experience (PX)**, *Feedback* refers to any information returned to the player about the consequences of their actions within the game system, while *Reward* is the positive reinforcement (virtual or psychological) delivered to the player upon successful execution of a goal, task, or progression milestone. Together, they form the core of the **Player Feedback Loop** — a closed-loop control mechanism that governs player engagement, motivation, skill acquisition, and long-term retention.

Mathematically, the feedback loop can be represented as a four-stage iterative cycle:

$$ \text{Player Action} \rightarrow \text{System Response} \rightarrow \text{Feedback Signal} \rightarrow \text{Player Cognition} \rightarrow \text{Next Action} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Feedback is the *language* of the game. Without feedback, the player is operating blind; without reward, the player has no reason to continue. Both must be **immediate, unambiguous, and meaningful**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Coffee Shop Analogy

Imagine you walk into a coffee shop and order a cappuccino. Here's how feedback and reward work in this real-world scenario:

| Stage | Real-World (Coffee Shop) | Game World Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Action** | You place an order | Player presses "Attack" button |
| **System Response** | Barista starts making coffee | Enemy health bar decreases |
| **Feedback** | Steam rises, machine hisses, cup slides across counter | Hit sound, screen shake, damage number appears |
| **Reward** | You sip a warm, delicious cappuccino | XP gained, loot drops, level-up sound |

Just as the coffee shop *must* tell you that your order is being prepared (otherwise you assume nothing is happening), the game *must* constantly communicate to the player what is happening as a result of their inputs. And just as the warm coffee makes you want to order again, the in-game reward makes the player want to play again.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Feedback answers *"What just happened?"* — Reward answers *"Why should I keep going?"*

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Player Continuation

The player continues playing due to three interlocking motivational pillars:

1. **Extrinsic Motivation** — Driven by external rewards (points, badges, leaderboards, unlockables).
2. **Intrinsic Motivation** — Driven by internal satisfaction (mastery, autonomy, competence, relatedness — per Self-Determination Theory).
3. **Feedback-Driven Adaptation** — The player adjusts strategy based on real-time feedback, creating a state of *flow*.

These pillars collectively ensure the player both *can* and *wants* to continue playing — the two essential conditions for player retention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Types of Feedback in Games

Game designers universally classify feedback into four distinct categories. Every interaction in a game delivers one or more of these:

#### 2.1.1 Visual Feedback
The most prevalent form. Includes:
- **HUD elements** (health bars, ammo counters, minimap markers)
- **Particle effects** (sparks, blood splatter, magic glows)
- **Color and lighting changes** (screen tints red when low HP)
- **Animation states** (enemy flinch, recoil, idle-to-running transition)
- **Screen shake and camera zoom** (impact, explosions)
- **UI popups** (+50 XP, "Quest Complete!")

#### 2.1.2 Auditory Feedback
- **SFX (Sound Effects)** — gunshots, footsteps, menu clicks
- **Music shifts** — tense combat music, peaceful exploration themes
- **Voice lines** — character barks ("Reloading!", "Enemy spotted!")
- **Audio cues** — rising pitch when low on health (the famous *Mario* heartbeat effect)

#### 2.1.3 Haptic Feedback (Tactile)
- **Controller vibration** — controller rumble on impact (DualSense, Xbox)
- **Adaptive triggers** — resistance changes based on weapon weight
- **Mobile vibration** — short buzz on collision or achievement

#### 2.1.4 Cognitive / Internal Feedback
- The *mental* feedback the player infers — e.g., seeing an enemy's shield disappear tells the player "I broke the shield, now I can deal damage."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When asked "list the types of feedback," always enumerate all four (Visual, Auditory, Haptic, Cognitive) and provide a game-based example for each. This is a frequently asked **2-mark** question.

### 2.2 The Reward Taxonomy

Rewards are classified along two axes: **Tangibility** and **Timing**.

#### By Tangibility:

| Reward Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Extrinsic (Tangible)** | Concrete, collectible items granted by the system | Gold coins, rare weapons, skins |
| **Intrinsic (Intangible)** | Psychological satisfaction from the activity itself | Sense of mastery, pride, immersion |

#### By Timing (Reinforcement Schedule — B.F. Skinner):

| Schedule | Description | Game Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous** | Reward after every action | Every kill gives XP |
| **Fixed Ratio** | Reward after N actions | Level up every 100 XP |
| **Variable Ratio** | Reward after unpredictable N actions | Loot box drops |
| **Fixed Interval** | Reward after fixed time | Daily login bonus |
| **Variable Interval** | Reward after unpredictable time | Random enemy encounters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Variable Ratio and Variable Interval schedules produce the highest and most persistent engagement** — this is why gacha games, slot machines, and randomized loot systems are so psychologically compelling (and potentially addictive).

### 2.3 Self-Determination Theory (SDT) Applied to Games

Psychologists **Deci & Ryan's Self-Determination Theory** identifies three innate psychological needs that drive intrinsic motivation. Modern game design heavily leverages these:

1. **Autonomy** — The player feels in control of their choices
   - *Game examples:* Branching dialogue, open-world exploration, character build freedom (RPG class systems)
2. **Competence** — The player feels they are mastering challenges
   - *Game examples:* Skill trees, progressive difficulty, clear feedback on improvement
3. **Relatedness** — The player feels connected to others
   - *Game examples:* Multiplayer co-op, guilds, NPC relationships, social leaderboards

A game that satisfies all three needs (e.g., *The Witcher 3*, *Stardew Valley*) generates *intrinsic* motivation that keeps players returning long after extrinsic rewards fade.

### 2.4 The Flow Channel (Csikszentmihalyi's Flow Theory)

Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi's **Flow Theory** states that optimal engagement occurs when **Challenge** matches **Skill Level**. If challenge exceeds skill → *anxiety*; if skill exceeds challenge → *boredom*.

$$ \text{Flow} = f(\text{Challenge}, \text{Skill}) \quad \text{where} \quad \text{Challenge} \approx \text{Skill} $$

The **Flow Channel** is the dynamic region in which the player experiences optimal engagement. Feedback and rewards are the *mechanisms* by which the game keeps the player inside this channel — feedback tells them how they're doing, rewards confirm their progress.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation / Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Feedback Loop Iteration | $F_{n+1} = R(A_n)$ | Next feedback derived from player's previous action response | — |
| 2 | Flow State Condition | $C \approx S$ | Challenge approximately equal to Skill | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Reinforcement Strength (Skinner) | $R_s = \frac{T_r}{T_t}$ | Rewards per unit time | Rewards/sec |
| 4 | Player Retention Decay | $P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential decay of active players | Players |
| 5 | Engagement Score | $E = \alpha F + \beta R + \gamma S$ | Weighted sum of Feedback, Reward, Skill match | Score |
| 6 | Variable Ratio Reward Expectation | $E[X] = \frac{N+1}{2}$ | Expected pulls to receive a reward (1 in N odds) | Pulls |
| 7 | Progression Curve | $XP_{n} = XP_{base} \cdot k^n$ | Exponential XP per level (k > 1) | XP |
| 8 | Hedonic Adaptation | $H(t) = H_{max} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Player habituates to constant reward over time | Happiness |
| 9 | Hedonic Threshold | $\Delta H = H(t+\Delta t) - H(t) < \epsilon$ | When reward-induced pleasure falls below threshold | Pleasure units |
| 10 | Difficulty Scaling | $D_{level} = D_0 \cdot (1 + r)^{L-1}$ | Difficulty per level (compound growth) | Difficulty pts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical for KTU Exams:** The Flow equation $C \approx S$ and the Hedonic Adaptation formula $H(t) = H_{max} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ are *high-yield*. Expect them in derivations or short-answer questions.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These principles are not confined to entertainment:
- **Educational software** (Duolingo, Khan Academy) uses variable ratio rewards to maintain learning streaks.
- **Fitness apps** (Strava, Fitbit) combine progress bars (feedback) with badges (rewards) to drive exercise adherence.
- **UI/UX design** in productivity tools (Notion, Trello) borrows game feedback patterns to make work satisfying.
- **Behavioral economics** research on gamification uses these models to design incentive systems in healthcare, finance, and workplace productivity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Player Feedback Loop as a Closed Control System

We model the player-game interaction as a **closed-loop control system**, analogous to a classical engineering feedback controller.

**Step 1: Define the System Variables**

Let:
- $A(t)$ = Player Action at time $t$ (input to the game)
- $S(A)$ = System Response function (how the game state changes)
- $F(S)$ = Feedback Signal (what the player perceives)
- $C(F)$ = Player Cognition (interpretation of feedback)
- $N(C)$ = Next Action decision

**Step 2: Establish the Causal Chain**

Each function depends on the previous:

$$
A(t) \xrightarrow{S(\cdot)} S(t) \xrightarrow{F(\cdot)} F(t) \xrightarrow{C(\cdot)} C(t) \xrightarrow{N(\cdot)} A(t+1)
$$

**Step 3: Express Iteratively**

$$
A(t+1) = N(C(F(S(A(t)))))
$$

**Step 4: Define the Composite Operator**

Let the composite operator $\Phi = N \circ C \circ F \circ S$. Then:

$$
A(t+1) = \Phi(A(t))
$$

**Step 5: Stability Analysis (Lyapunov-Style)**

For the system to be *engaging* (stable with bounded oscillation around an optimal trajectory), the operator $\Phi$ must map actions to a bounded region. The fixed point $A^*$ satisfies:

$$
A^* = \Phi(A^*)
$$

**Step 6: Perturbation Analysis**

Let $A(t) = A^* + \delta(t)$ where $\delta$ is a small perturbation. Linearizing around the fixed point:

$$
\delta(t+1) = \Phi'(A^*) \cdot \delta(t)
$$

**Step 7: Convergence Condition**

For the player to settle into a stable engagement pattern, we require $\vert \Phi'(A^*) \vert < 1$ (the derivative magnitude must be less than unity). If $\vert \Phi'(A^*) \vert \geq 1$, the player becomes either *frustrated* (oscillation grows) or *bored* (system diverges monotonically).

$$
\boxed{\text{Stable Engagement} \iff \vert \Phi'(A^*) \vert < 1}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Good game design ensures that small deviations from the optimal action are *corrected* by the feedback-reward system, pulling the player back into the flow channel.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Player Retention Decay

**Step 1: Empirical Observation**

Cohort analysis of live-service games shows that daily active players decay roughly exponentially after launch.

**Step 2: Assume First-Order Decay**

Let $P(t)$ = number of active players at time $t$. The decay rate is proportional to current population:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = -\lambda P(t)
$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is the decay constant (units: 1/day).

**Step 3: Solve the ODE**

Separating variables:

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = -\lambda \, dt
$$

**Step 4: Integrate Both Sides**

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P(t)} \frac{dP}{P} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate**

$$
\ln\left(\frac{P(t)}{P_0}\right) = -\lambda t
$$

**Step 6: Exponentiate**

$$
P(t) = P_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 7: Compute Half-Life**

Setting $P(t_{1/2}) = P_0 / 2$:

$$
t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 8: Game Design Implication**

To *flatten* the decay curve, designers introduce **feedback and reward interventions** (events, new content, daily quests). Mathematically, this corresponds to adding a *forcing term*:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = -\lambda P(t) + R(t)
$$

where $R(t)$ is the re-engagement reward injection rate.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Hedonic Adaptation and Reward Novelty

**Step 1: Define Pleasure Function**

Let $H(t)$ = hedonic pleasure (satisfaction) the player derives from a constant reward. Initially $H(0) = 0$.

**Step 2: Empirical Model**

Psychological research shows pleasure grows toward a maximum $H_{max}$ but with diminishing returns, and habituates over time:

$$
\frac{dH}{dt} = k \cdot (H_{max} - H(t)) - \mu H(t)
$$

where $k$ is the learning rate and $\mu$ is the habituation rate.

**Step 3: Simplify**

$$
\frac{dH}{dt} = (k H_{max}) - (k + \mu) H(t)
$$

**Step 4: Solve the Linear ODE**

The general solution is:

$$
H(t) = \frac{k H_{max}}{k + \mu} \left(1 - e^{-(k+\mu)t}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Asymptote**

As $t \to \infty$, $H(t) \to H_{max} \cdot \frac{k}{k + \mu}$ — the *effective* maximum pleasure, which is *less* than $H_{max}$ due to habituation.

**Step 6: Design Implication**

To prevent the player from habituating, designers must periodically **increase reward novelty** (new items, new challenges, new mechanics). This resets $H(t)$ by increasing $H_{max}$ or $k$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Feedback-Reward Mechanisms Across Game Genres

| Genre | Primary Feedback | Primary Reward | Continuation Driver |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FPS (Call of Duty)** | Hit markers, kill feed, hit sound | XP, rank, unlockable weapons | Competence + status |
| **RPG (Elden Ring)** | Damage numbers, level-up chime, soul counter | New gear, stat growth | Mastery + exploration |
| **Puzzle (Tetris)** | Line-clear flash, score pop | High score, level speed-up | Competence (flow) |
| **MMO (World of Warcraft)** | Quest-complete UI, loot beam | Gear, social bonds | Relatedness + progression |
| **Mobile (Candy Crush)** | Confetti, "Sweet!" sound | New levels, lives | Variable ratio + streaks |
| **Sandbox (Minecraft)** | Block break particles, day/night | Creative freedom | Autonomy + creativity |
| **Roguelike (Hades)** | Death screen, boon selection | Meta-progression, story unlock | Competence + narrative |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Core Player Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Player Action] --> B[Game Engine Response]
    B --> C[Visual Feedback]
    B --> D[Audio Feedback]
    B --> E[Haptic Feedback]
    C --> F[Player Perception]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Cognitive Interpretation]
    G --> H{Reward Earned?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Reward Granted]
    H -- No --> J[Negative Feedback / Hint]
    I --> K[Player Motivation Boost]
    J --> K
    K --> A
```

### 4.2 Self-Determination Theory Triangle in Game Design

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SDT[Self Determination Theory Core Needs]
        AUT[Autonomy]
        COM[Competence]
        REL[Relatedness]
    end
    AUT --> IM[Intrinsic Motivation]
    COM --> IM
    REL --> IM
    IM --> ENG[Player Engagement]
    ENG --> RET[Long Term Retention]
    subgraph EXR[Extrinsic Rewards]
        EXT[Points Badges Loot]
    end
    EXT --> ENG
    EXT -.supplements.-> IM
```

### 4.3 Flow Channel and Difficulty-Skill Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        BOR[Boredom Region]
    end

    end

        ANX[Anxiety Region]
    end
    SKILL[Player Skill Level] --> FLOW
    CHAL[Challenge Level] --> FLOW
    FEED[Feedback System] -->|Adjusts Challenge| CHAL
    REW[Reward System] -->|Boosts Skill| SKILL
    LOW --> FLOW
    HIGH --> FLOW
    FLOW --> PLAYER[Engaged Player]
```

### 4.4 Reinforcement Schedule Comparison (Skinner Box Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    R[Reinforcement Schedules] --> CR[Continuous]
    R --> FR[Fixed Ratio]
    R --> VR[Variable Ratio]
    R --> FI[Fixed Interval]
    R --> VI[Variable Interval]
    CR -->|Every Action| EX1[Lowest persistence]
    FR -->|Every Nth Action| EX2[Moderate pauses]
    VR -->|Random Nth Action| EX3[Highest persistence Gacha games]

```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Reward System

```mermaid

    end

    end

        META[Meta Progression Unlocker]

    EVT --> DET
    DET --> RULES
    RULES --> SCHED
    SCHED --> VAL
    VAL --> FEED
    VAL --> DB
    DB --> META
    META -->|New Content Unlocked| EVT
```
