topics.title: Layouts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT745 - GAME DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Layouts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Game Development**, a **Layout** refers to the structured spatial arrangement of graphical user interface (GUI) elements, menus, heads-up displays (HUDs), and virtual objects on a defined game canvas or viewport. A layout system defines **how UI elements are positioned, sized, aligned, anchored, and scaled** relative to a parent container, screen edge, reference object, or resolution target.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Layouts are treated as a fundamental component of the **Game Interface Architecture** layer, sitting between the rendering pipeline and the user input system. The layout subsystem is responsible for translating **abstract UI hierarchies** (defined in the editor) into **concrete pixel coordinates** during the frame composition stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — PEAIT745 / Module 4**
> Layouts govern the deterministic placement of HUD widgets, menu screens, and in-world elements, ensuring visual consistency across multiple device resolutions, aspect ratios, and orientations (portrait / landscape).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for First-Time Learners

Imagine a **movie theatre lobby**. There is a **big poster on the wall** (relative anchor), a **ticket counter on the left** (fixed anchor), a **digital display board hanging from the ceiling** (parented layout), and a **seating chart on a stand** (grid layout). The theatre manager does not re-measure the room for every visitor; instead, each element has a **rule** that says *where it belongs*.

A **Game Layout System** works identically. Each UI element (health bar, score, minimap, button) carries a **rule** — a directive such as *"stick to the top-left corner"*, *"stretch across the screen width"*, or *"center horizontally"*. The layout engine evaluates these rules every frame and computes the final pixel positions.

## 1.3 Why Layouts Matter in Game Development

| Reason | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|
| **Multi-Resolution Support** | Games must run on 1080p monitors, 4K TVs, mobile screens, and tablets. Layouts decouple logic from pixel coordinates. |
| **Aspect Ratio Variation** | 16:9, 21:9, 4:3 all demand adaptive positioning of HUD elements. |
| **Localization** | Text in German is longer than English; layouts must accommodate dynamic text expansion. |
| **Orientation Switching** | Mobile games toggle portrait/landscape; layouts recalculate anchors automatically. |
| **Designer Productivity** | Designers place elements in normalized space instead of computing pixel offsets manually. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard Reference**
> Modern engines (Unity UI Canvas, Unreal UMG, Godot Control Nodes) all implement layout systems that follow the same conceptual pillars: **Anchor, Pivot, Rect Transform, and Constraint System**.

## 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot

- **Canvas** — The root virtual coordinate space on which all UI elements are drawn.
- **Anchor** — A reference point (or set of points) on the parent that the element is attached to.
- **Pivot** — The element's own internal origin used for rotation and scaling.
- **Rect Transform** — A 2D rectangle (position, size, rotation) describing an element's geometry.
- **Constraint** — A rule limiting how an element may resize (e.g., maintain aspect ratio).
- **Stretch** — A layout mode where an element expands to fill the gap between two anchors.
- **Safe Area** — A region guaranteed to be visible on all devices (notches, rounded corners).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of a Layout System

A robust game layout architecture is built upon four interlocking pillars. Mastering these is the single most high-yield action a KTU 2024 student can take for this module.

### Pillar 1 — The Canvas (Root Coordinate Space)

The **Canvas** is the topmost virtual plane of the user interface. Every UI element is a descendant of the canvas. The canvas defines the **reference resolution** — the pixel dimension on which all subsequent calculations are normalized.

Let:
- $W_{ref}$ = Reference canvas width (in pixels)
- $H_{ref}$ = Reference canvas height (in pixels)

A child element's **normalized position** is then a fractional value in $[0, 1]$ across this reference space.

### Pillar 2 — Anchors

An **anchor** is a 2D coordinate $(a_x, a_y)$ expressed as a fraction of the parent's dimensions, where $a_x \in [0, 1]$ and $a_y \in [0, 1]$.

| Anchor Mode | Behaviour | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Top-Left Fixed** | Element stays at $(0,1)$ anchor | Notification badges |
| **Center Fixed** | Element stays at $(0.5, 0.5)$ | Logo, splash titles |
| **Bottom-Stretch** | Element stretches along X-axis | Action buttons, chat bars |
| **Full-Stretch** | Element fills entire parent | Background panels, modals |

### Pillar 3 — Pivots

The **pivot** $P = (p_x, p_y)$ specifies which point on the element itself is treated as the "handle" for positioning, rotation, and scaling. A pivot of $(0.5, 0.5)$ means the element rotates around its own visual center.

### Pillar 4 — Rect Transform Geometry

The actual on-screen rectangle is computed every frame using the following foundational relationships. These are the equations most often tested in KTU board examinations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every layout equation a student is expected to know for PEAIT745 Module 4. Memorize the symbols; understand the geometry.

| Concept | Symbol | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Anchor Vector | $A$ | $A = (a_x, a_y), \; a_x,a_y \in [0,1]$ | Expressed in parent-normalized space |
| Pivot Vector | $P$ | $P = (p_x, p_y), \; p_x,p_y \in [0,1]$ | Expressed in element-local space |
| Parent Size | $S_{par}$ | $S_{par} = (W_{par}, H_{par})$ | In pixels |
| Anchor World Position | $A_{px}$ | $A_{px} = (a_x \cdot W_{par}, \; a_y \cdot H_{par})$ | Convert anchor fraction to pixels |
| Element Position (offset) | $X_{elem}$ | $X_{elem} = A_{px} + (o_x, o_y)$ | $o_x, o_y$ = offset from anchor |
| Stretch Width | $W_{stretch}$ | $W_{stretch} = (a_{x2} - a_{x1}) \cdot W_{par} + o_w$ | Two horizontal anchors |
| Stretch Height | $H_{stretch}$ | $H_{stretch} = (a_{y2} - a_{y1}) \cdot H_{par} + o_h$ | Two vertical anchors |
| Reference Resolution Scale | $k_{scale}$ | $k_{scale} = \min(W_{scr} / W_{ref},\; H_{scr} / H_{ref})$ | Match-mode (uniform scale) |
| Aspect Ratio | $\rho$ | $\rho = W_{scr} / H_{scr}$ | Screen aspect ratio |
| Layout Pixels-per-Unit | $PPU$ | $PPU = W_{ref} / U_{ref}$ | $U_{ref}$ = reference units |

> **Critical LaTeX Note for Students**
> When writing $\vert a_x \vert$ in your answer scripts, use the `\vert` command — never the raw `|` symbol — to avoid markdown rendering glitches in the digital valuation portal.

## 2.3 Anchor-Pivot Mathematics in Detail

The full layout computation pipeline proceeds in three deterministic stages each frame.

### Stage A — Resolve Anchor in Parent Space

The anchor is a fractional position. To convert it to actual pixel coordinates inside the parent rectangle, multiply each component by the parent dimension:

$$A_{px} = (a_x \cdot W_{par}, \; a_y \cdot H_{par})$$

### Stage B — Apply Offset

The designer specifies an offset $(o_x, o_y)$ which displaces the element's pivot from the anchor:

$$X_{elem} = A_{px} + (o_x, o_y)$$

### Stage C — Apply Pivot-Centred Bounding Box

The element has a measured size $(W_{elem}, H_{elem})$. To draw it so that the **pivot** lands at $X_{elem}$, the top-left rendering coordinate becomes:

$$X_{render} = X_{elem} - (p_x \cdot W_{elem}, \; p_y \cdot H_{elem})$$

This three-stage process is the heart of the Rect Transform system and is a guaranteed KTU examination question.

## 2.4 Reference Resolution Scaling Modes

When a game's actual screen size $S_{scr} = (W_{scr}, H_{scr})$ differs from the reference resolution $S_{ref} = (W_{ref}, H_{ref})$, the layout engine applies one of three scaling modes.

### 2.4.1 Constant Pixel Size (No Scaling)

The element retains its authored pixel dimensions regardless of screen. The on-screen size remains $W_{elem}$.

$$k_{scale} = 1.0$$

### 2.4.2 Scale with Screen Size (Match)

A uniform scalar is applied so that the entire reference canvas fits the smaller of the two screen dimensions without letterboxing:

$$k_{scale} = \min\!\left(\frac{W_{scr}}{W_{ref}}, \frac{H_{scr}}{H_{ref}}\right)$$

The on-screen element size becomes:

$$W_{elem}^{on} = k_{scale} \cdot W_{elem}$$

### 2.4.3 Expand (Match Width or Height)

A weighted blend is used, controlled by a parameter $m \in [0,1]$ representing "match width or height":

$$k_{scale} = \frac{W_{scr}}{W_{ref}} \cdot m + \frac{H_{scr}}{H_{ref}} \cdot (1 - m)$$

Setting $m = 0$ matches height, $m = 1$ matches width, and $m = 0.5$ gives a balanced diagonal fit.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile Game Studios** rely on layout systems to ship a single UI asset that runs on iPhone SE (640×1136) and iPad Pro 12.9 (2048×2732) without per-device redesign.
- **AAA Console Studios** use stretch anchors for cinematic letterbox bars to preserve 21:9 director vision on 16:9 panels.
- **VR/AR Systems** adopt world-space layouts where elements are anchored to 3D points rather than screen edges, but the underlying Rect Transform math is preserved.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Anchor-Offset-Pivot Pixel Position

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Given a parent canvas of pixel size $(W_{par}, H_{par}) = (1920, 1080)$, a child UI element with:

- Anchor $A = (0.0, 1.0)$ (top-left corner of parent)
- Offset $(o_x, o_y) = (40, -40)$ (40 px right, 40 px down from anchor)
- Pivot $P = (0.0, 1.0)$ (top-left of element)
- Element size $(W_{elem}, H_{elem}) = (320, 80)$ (a health bar)

Compute the **top-left pixel coordinate** at which the renderer must draw the element.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Convert the anchor fraction to a pixel position inside the parent.**

Since the anchor is at the top-left of the parent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{px,x} &= a_x \cdot W_{par} \\
&= 0.0 \cdot 1920 \\
&= 0 \text{ px} \\
A_{px,y} &= a_y \cdot H_{par} \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 1080 \\
&= 1080 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the anchor sits at the pixel $(0, 1080)$.

**Step 2 — Apply the offset to find the pivot's world position.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{elem,x} &= A_{px,x} + o_x \\
&= 0 + 40 \\
&= 40 \text{ px} \\
X_{elem,y} &= A_{px,y} + o_y \\
&= 1080 + (-40) \\
&= 1040 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

The pivot of the health bar should land at pixel $(40, 1040)$ on the canvas.

**Step 3 — Convert the pivot's world position into the element's top-left render position.**

Since the pivot is the top-left corner of the element ($p_x = p_y = 0$), no subtraction is needed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{render,x} &= X_{elem,x} - p_x \cdot W_{elem} \\
&= 40 - 0.0 \cdot 320 \\
&= 40 \text{ px} \\
X_{render,y} &= X_{elem,y} - p_y \cdot H_{elem} \\
&= 1040 - 0.0 \cdot 80 \\
&= 1040 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer.**

The renderer must draw the health bar starting at pixel coordinate $(40, 1040)$ and extending to $(40 + 320, 1040 + 80) = (360, 1120)$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation — Stretch Anchor Width

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

A bottom action bar is configured with a **stretch anchor**:

- Left anchor $a_{x1} = 0.0$
- Right anchor $a_{x2} = 1.0$
- Vertical anchor $a_y = 0.0$ (bottom edge)
- Vertical offset $o_y = 20$ px
- Fixed height $H_{elem} = 80$ px

Parent canvas: $1920 \times 1080$.

Compute the final stretched width and top-left render coordinate.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the anchor span in pixels.**

The horizontal anchor range is $a_{x2} - a_{x1} = 1.0 - 0.0 = 1.0$.

**Step 2 — Convert to stretched width in pixels.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{stretch} &= (a_{x2} - a_{x1}) \cdot W_{par} + o_w \\
&= 1.0 \cdot 1920 + 0 \\
&= 1920 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

The action bar is now full-width (1920 px) by design.

**Step 3 — Compute vertical anchor pixel position.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{px,y} &= a_y \cdot H_{par} \\
&= 0.0 \cdot 1080 \\
&= 0 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply vertical offset and height to find top render row.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{render,y} &= A_{px,y} + o_y \\
&= 0 + 20 \\
&= 20 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

The action bar's top edge sits at $y = 20$ px; the bar extends 80 px downward, ending at $y = 100$ px. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation — Reference Resolution Scale Factor

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

A game is authored at reference resolution $(W_{ref}, H_{ref}) = (1920, 1080)$. It runs on a monitor with $(W_{scr}, H_{scr}) = (2560, 1440)$. Compute the **Match** scale factor $k_{scale}$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute width ratio.**

$$
\frac{W_{scr}}{W_{ref}} = \frac{2560}{1920} = 1.3333
$$

**Step 2 — Compute height ratio.**

$$
\frac{H_{scr}}{H_{ref}} = \frac{1440}{1080} = 1.3333
$$

**Step 3 — Take the minimum (Match mode).**

$$
k_{scale} = \min(1.3333, 1.3333) = 1.3333
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

Every UI element drawn at 1 px in the authoring tool becomes $1.3333$ px on screen, yielding a uniform 1.333× (4K-equivalent) layout. The aspect ratio is preserved at $16:9$. ✓

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Layout Mode Selection

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix in design viva and Part B answers. KTU examiners award marks for justification, not just stating the answer.

| Layout Goal | Recommended Anchor | Recommended Pivot | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pause menu centred on any screen | Center $(0.5, 0.5)$ | Center $(0.5, 0.5)$ | Symmetric scaling in both axes |
| Health bar in top-left, never resizes | Top-Left $(0, 1)$ | Top-Left $(0, 1)$ | Zero-margin badge, fixed corner |
| Bottom action bar spans all widths | Stretch $(0, 0)$ to $(1, 0)$ | Top-Center $(0.5, 1)$ | Width adapts, vertical stays pinned |
| Score number floats above enemy | Custom $(0.5, 1)$ | Center $(0.5, 0.5)$ | Hovers, rotation-friendly |
| Background image fills screen | Stretch $(0,0)-(1,1)$ | Center $(0.5, 0.5)$ | Aspect-aware, no gaps |
| Dialogue box anchored to NPC | Parent-relative | Center $(0.5, 0.5)$ | World-space speech bubble |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style (End-to-End)

A minimap widget is configured as follows:

- Parent canvas: $1920 \times 1080$
- Anchor: $(1.0, 1.0)$ — top-right corner
- Offset: $(-20, -20)$ — 20 px inset from corner
- Pivot: $(1.0, 1.0)$ — top-right of element
- Element size: $200 \times 200$ (square minimap)

**Step 1** — Convert anchor to pixels:

$$
A_{px} = (1.0 \cdot 1920, \; 1.0 \cdot 1080) = (1920, 1080)
$$

**Step 2** — Apply offset:

$$
X_{elem} = (1920 - 20, \; 1080 - 20) = (1900, 1060)
$$

**Step 3** — Convert pivot to render top-left:

$$
X_{render} = (1900 - 1.0 \cdot 200, \; 1060 - 1.0 \cdot 200) = (1700, 860)
$$

**Step 4** — Final bounding box: $(1700, 860)$ to $(1900, 1060)$. ✓

The minimap occupies a 200×200 square in the top-right corner with a 20 px breathing margin.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Layout Computation Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parent Canvas Wpar x Hpar] --> B[Anchor Fraction ax ay]
    B --> C[Anchor in Pixels Apx = ax Wpar, ay Hpar]
    D[Designer Offset ox oy] --> E[Pivot World Position Xelem]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Apply Pivot: Xrender = Xelem minus p Welem, p Helem]
    G[Element Size Welem x Helem] --> F
    F --> H[Final Render Rectangle on GPU]
    I[Reference Resolution Wref x Href] --> J[Scale Mode Selection]
    J --> K{Constant / Match / Expand}
    K --> H
    L[Safe Area Inset] --> H
    H --> M[Composed Frame Output]
```

## 4.2 Anchor-Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Choose Anchor] --> B{Does element need to stay at a fixed screen corner?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Select Corner Anchor 0,0 0,1 1,0 or 1,1]
    B -- No --> D{Does element need to scale with screen size?}
    D -- No --> E[Center Anchor 0.5, 0.5]
    D -- Yes --> F{Stretch in one axis or both?}
    F -- One axis --> G[Two-point Horizontal or Vertical Stretch]
    F -- Both axes --> H[Four-corner Full Stretch 0,0 to 1,1]
    C --> I[Apply Offset and Pivot]
    E --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Final Layout Output]
```

## 4.3 Resolution-Scale Mode Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Screen Resolution Wscr x Hscr] --> B[Reference Resolution Wref x Href]
    B --> C{Difference detected?}
    C -- None --> D[Constant Pixel Mode kscale equals 1]
    C -- Width or Height differs --> E[Match Mode kscale equals min Wscr/Wref, Hscr/Href]
    C -- Mixed aspect ratio --> F[Expand Mode kscale equals weighted blend m]
    D --> G[Apply kscale to all elements]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Layout Resolved for Frame]
```

## 4.4 UI Element Hierarchy (Composite Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Canvas 1920 x 1080] --> B[Top Bar Container]
    A --> C[Center Menu Container]
    A --> D[Bottom Action Bar Container]
    A --> E[Safe Area Overlay]
    B --> B1[Health Bar Anchor 0,1]
    B --> B2[Score Text Anchor 1,1]
    C --> C1[Title Image Anchor 0.5,0.5]
    C --> C2[Start Button Anchor 0.5,0.5]
    D --> D1[Inventory Slot Grid Anchor 0,0 to 1,0]
    D --> D2[Chat Input Field Anchor 0,0 to 1,0]
    E --> E1[Notch Mask Anchor 0,1 to 1,1]
```

## 4.5 Pivot vs Anchor — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anchor on Parent] --> B[Reference Point in Parent Space]
    C[Pivot on Element] --> D[Handle Point in Element Space]
    B --> E[Position Computed Each Frame]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Render Rectangle on Canvas]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip for KTU Viva**
> The anchor is **not** on the element — it is on the **parent**. The pivot is **not** on the parent — it is on the **element**. Confusing these two is the most common viva deduction.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a Layout in the context of game development. List any four key properties that characterize a UI layout system.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Layout** in game development is the structured spatial arrangement of UI elements — such as HUDs, menus, and notifications — on a game canvas according to predefined positional rules. It governs where elements are drawn and how they behave when the screen size, aspect ratio, or orientation changes.

The four key properties of a layout system are:

1. **Anchor** — defines the reference point on the parent container.
2. **Pivot** — defines the element's internal handle for transformation.
3. **Rect Transform** — describes the element's pixel rectangle, size, and rotation.
4. **Constraint / Stretch Mode** — defines whether the element scales with the parent or stays fixed.

> **Examiner's Note (3-mark allocation):** Definition (1 mark) + Four properties with one-line description (2 marks). Students commonly lose 0.5 marks by writing the anchor definition incorrectly as "a point on the element."

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between Anchor and Pivot. Why is this distinction important in game UI design?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Anchor | Pivot |
|---|---|---|
| **Defined on** | Parent container | Element itself |
| **Coordinate space** | Parent-normalized $[0,1]$ | Element-local $[0,1]$ |
| **Purpose** | Positions the element relative to the parent | Defines rotation/scaling handle |
| **Moves with parent** | Yes | No (stays fixed to element) |

This distinction is important because the **anchor** determines **where the element goes** on the screen, while the **pivot** determines **how the element transforms** (rotates, scales) once placed. Conflating them causes layout drift, off-center rotations, and visual bugs during resolution changes.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions carry **14 marks each** and consist of two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The internal choice must be **structurally independent** — i.e., the student picks one full question.

---

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the Rect Transform system used in modern game engines. Describe the role of anchors, pivots, and offsets with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **Rect Transform** is the layout component that describes a 2D UI element's rectangle in a game engine's UI system (e.g., Unity, Unreal UMG, Godot). Unlike the regular Transform (which is anchored at the geometric center for 3D objects), the Rect Transform carries **additional layout metadata** — anchor, pivot, and offset — that allows the element to behave predictably across different screen sizes.

**Components of Rect Transform:**

1. **Anchor ($A = (a_x, a_y)$)**: A 2D point in parent-normalized space, where each component is a fraction $[0,1]$ of the parent's width and height respectively. The anchor tells the engine *where on the parent* the element is conceptually attached. Common anchor presets are top-left, center, and bottom-right.

2. **Pivot ($P = (p_x, p_y)$)**: A 2D point in element-local space that defines which point of the element is used for positional calculations, rotation, and scaling. A pivot of $(0.5, 0.5)$ is the visual center; $(0, 0)$ is the bottom-left.

3. **Offset ($(o_x, o_y)$)**: A pixel displacement applied between the anchor's pixel position and the element's pivot. The designer controls this to position the element with sub-anchor precision.

**Pipeline of Calculation (per frame):**

**Step 1** — Convert the anchor fraction into pixel coordinates inside the parent:

$$A_{px} = (a_x \cdot W_{par}, \; a_y \cdot H_{par})$$

**Step 2** — Add the offset to obtain the world position of the pivot:

$$X_{elem} = A_{px} + (o_x, o_y)$$

**Step 3** — Subtract pivot contribution to find the top-left render coordinate:

$$X_{render} = X_{elem} - (p_x \cdot W_{elem}, \; p_y \cdot H_{elem})$$

**Neat Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Parent Canvas 1920x1080] --> B[Anchor at 0,1 Top-Left]
    B --> C[Anchor Pixel Apx = 0, 1080]
    D[Offset 40, -40] --> E[Pivot World 40, 1040]
    C --> E
    E --> F[Pivot at 0,0 on Element]
    F --> G[Render Top-Left equals 40, 1040]
    G --> H[Element 320x80 Drawn on Screen]
```

**Conclusion:**
The Rect Transform separates the concerns of *positioning* (anchor), *transforming* (pivot), and *fine-tuning* (offset). This separation is what enables a single UI design to deploy across phones, tablets, consoles, and PC monitors without per-device authoring.

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Definition of Rect Transform (1) + Anchor / Pivot / Offset explanation (3) + Calculation pipeline (2) + Diagram (1).

---

#### (b) A game is authored at reference resolution 1920×1080 and runs on a 2560×1440 monitor. Compute the scale factor using the "Match" mode. If a UI button has an authored size of 200×50 pixels, what is its on-screen size? Explain what happens if the screen aspect ratio changes from 16:9 to 21:9. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Match scale factor $k_{scale}$.**

For "Match" mode, the engine uses the smaller of the two ratios to avoid cropping:

$$
k_{scale} = \min\!\left(\frac{W_{scr}}{W_{ref}}, \frac{H_{scr}}{H_{ref}}\right)
$$

Computing each ratio:

$$
\frac{W_{scr}}{W_{ref}} = \frac{2560}{1920} = 1.3333
$$

$$
\frac{H_{scr}}{H_{ref}} = \frac{1440}{1080} = 1.3333
$$

The minimum is $k_{scale} = 1.3333$.

**Step 2 — Compute the on-screen size of the button.**

$$
W_{button}^{on} = k_{scale} \cdot W_{button} = 1.3333 \cdot 200 = 266.67 \text{ px}
$$

$$
H_{button}^{on} = k_{scale} \cdot H_{button} = 1.3333 \cdot 50 = 66.67 \text{ px}
$$

The on-screen button is $266.67 \times 66.67$ pixels.

**Step 3 — Behaviour under aspect ratio change (16:9 → 21:9).**

The new screen is, say, $2560 \times 1080$ (ultrawide 21:9). The ratios become:

$$
\frac{W_{scr}}{W_{ref}} = \frac{2560}{1920} = 1.3333
$$

$$
\frac{H_{scr}}{H_{ref}} = \frac{1080}{1080} = 1.0000
$$

The Match scale becomes $k_{scale} = \min(1.3333, 1.0000) = 1.0000$.

**Implications:**

- The **vertical** scale is preserved at 1.0 — elements maintain their height.
- The **horizontal** scale is suppressed — ultrawide screens do **not** stretch elements wider.
- The unused horizontal space is treated as **letterbox margin** on the left and right of the canvas.
- Anchored elements (center, top-left) remain in their expected positions, and stretch elements grow only as much as the smaller dimension permits.

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Correct formula (1) + Numerical calculation (2) + On-screen size (1) + Aspect-ratio analysis (2) + Letterbox explanation (1).

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### (a) Discuss the three primary scaling modes used by game engines to handle different screen resolutions. Compare their behaviour on a 4K vs Full HD display. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Modern game engines offer three primary scaling modes for UI canvases. The choice of mode determines how the UI reacts when the screen resolution differs from the design-time reference.

**Mode 1 — Constant Pixel Size:**

In this mode, the engine does **not** apply any scaling. UI elements retain their authored pixel size regardless of the actual screen dimensions.

- On 1920×1080: A 200 px button renders at 200 px.
- On 3840×2160: The same 200 px button renders at 200 px (visually **smaller** relative to the screen).
- **Use case:** Pixel-art games, UI overlays that must remain a fixed size (tooltips, cursor hovers).

**Mode 2 — Scale with Screen Size (Match):**

The engine computes a uniform scalar $k_{scale}$ using the minimum of the two dimension ratios, then applies it to every UI element.

$$
k_{scale} = \min\!\left(\frac{W_{scr}}{W_{ref}}, \frac{H_{scr}}{H_{ref}}\right)
$$

- On 1920×1080 (with $W_{ref} = 1920, H_{ref} = 1080$): $k_{scale} = 1.0$.
- On 3840×2160: $k_{scale} = 2.0$. The 200 px button becomes 400 px on screen, looking proportionally identical to the Full HD case.
- **Use case:** General-purpose games that must look identical across HD and 4K.

**Mode 3 — Expand (Match Width or Height):**

A weighted blend is computed using a designer-controlled parameter $m \in [0,1]$:

$$
k_{scale} = \frac{W_{scr}}{W_{ref}} \cdot m + \frac{H_{scr}}{H_{ref}} \cdot (1 - m)
$$

- $m = 0$ → height-matched: width-wise growth is unrestricted (useful for 4:3 → 16:9).
- $m = 1$ → width-matched: height-wise growth is unrestricted.
- $m = 0.5$ → balanced diagonal scaling.
- **Use case:** Tabletop games, board games, sims that want to fill the screen on exotic aspect ratios.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Constant Pixel | Match | Expand |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4K uniform scaling | No | Yes (k=2) | Configurable |
| Aspect ratio preserved | Always | Always (with letterbox) | Tunable |
| Designer effort | Low | Low | High (tune $m$) |
| Letterbox risk | No | Yes (ultrawide) | Depends on $m$ |

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Three modes named and defined (3) + Formulas (2) + Comparison table (1) + Use cases (1).

---

#### (b) A minimap UI element has parent canvas $1920 \times 1080$, anchor $(1.0, 1.0)$, offset $(-20, -20)$, pivot $(1.0, 1.0)$, and size $200 \times 200$. Compute the top-left render coordinate step by step. What will happen to the minimap's position if the screen resolution is changed to $1280 \times 720$ while the same anchor / offset / pivot are used? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert anchor fraction to pixel position in parent.**

The anchor is the top-right corner of the parent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{px,x} &= a_x \cdot W_{par} = 1.0 \cdot 1920 = 1920 \text{ px} \\
A_{px,y} &= a_y \cdot H_{par} = 1.0 \cdot 1080 = 1080 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the offset to obtain the pivot's world position.**

The offset $(-20, -20)$ shifts the pivot 20 px left and 20 px up from the anchor — a standard 20 px inset from the top-right corner.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{elem,x} &= 1920 + (-20) = 1900 \text{ px} \\
X_{elem,y} &= 1080 + (-20) = 1060 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert the pivot's world position into the top-left render coordinate.**

Since the pivot is the top-right of the element ($p_x = p_y = 1.0$), we subtract the full element size:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{render,x} &= 1900 - 1.0 \cdot 200 = 1700 \text{ px} \\
X_{render,y} &= 1060 - 1.0 \cdot 200 = 860 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final bounding box on Full HD.**

The minimap occupies the rectangle from $(1700, 860)$ to $(1900, 1060)$ on the 1920×1080 canvas. It appears as a 200×200 square hugging the top-right corner with a 20 px margin. ✓

**Step 5 — Recompute on 1280×720 screen (same anchor and offset).**

Because the anchor and offset are **parent-relative fractions** (anchor) and **pixel offsets** (offset), only the parent size changes. Re-running the calculation with $W_{par} = 1280, H_{par} = 720$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{px,x} &= 1.0 \cdot 1280 = 1280 \text{ px} \\
A_{px,y} &= 1.0 \cdot 720 = 720 \text{ px} \\
X_{elem,x} &= 1280 - 20 = 1260 \text{ px} \\
X_{elem,y} &= 720 - 20 = 700 \text{ px} \\
X_{render,x} &= 1260 - 200 = 1060 \text{ px} \\
X_{render,y} &= 700 - 200 = 500 \text{ px}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Behavioural Analysis.**

- The minimap **remains anchored to the top-right corner** on both resolutions.
- The **20 px inset is preserved** in absolute pixel terms (the offset is not scaled).
- The **minimap's own size remains 200×200** in raw pixels because no canvas scale mode is applied.
- However, the minimap now occupies a **larger fraction of the 720p screen** (200/1280 ≈ 15.6% of the width) compared to Full HD (200/1920 ≈ 10.4% of the width).
- If a uniform scale mode (Match) had been applied, the minimap would be scaled *down* on 720p to maintain visual proportion, keeping the relative size constant across resolutions.

> **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Step-by-step pixel calculation on Full HD (3) + Correct identification of all three formulas (2) + Recomputation on 720p + interpretation (2).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing Anchor and Pivot:** Many students treat them as the same point. Anchor is on the **parent**; pivot is on the **element**. KTU examiners specifically look for this distinction in Part B (a) questions.
> 2. **Forgetting Pivot Subtraction:** When asked for the *render* coordinate, students often stop at the pivot's world position. The render coordinate requires the pivot subtraction ($-p_x \cdot W_{elem}$). This error costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 3. **Using Maximum Instead of Minimum in Match Mode:** The Match scale factor uses $\min$, not $\max$. Using $\max$ would crop content on the shorter dimension.
> 4. **Treating Offset as a Fraction:** Offsets are in **pixels**, not in $[0,1]$ normalized space. Mixing these units is a guaranteed mark deduction.
> 5. **Ignoring Pivot-as-Center Convention:** A pivot of $(0.5, 0.5)$ is the visual center. A button rotating around $(0, 0)$ swings wildly; rotating around $(0.5, 0.5)$ spins in place.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Layouts (PEAIT745 / Module 4)**

- **Layout** = structured spatial arrangement of UI elements on a canvas with deterministic positioning rules.
- **Canvas** = root virtual coordinate space; defines the **reference resolution** $(W_{ref}, H_{ref})$.
- **Anchor** = fractional position $(a_x, a_y) \in [0,1]^2$ on the **parent** that the element is attached to.
- **Pivot** = fractional position $(p_x, p_y) \in [0,1]^2$ on the **element** used as the transformation handle.
- **Offset** = pixel displacement $(o_x, o_y)$ between anchor and pivot; expressed in **pixels, not fractions**.
- **Rect Transform** = the 2D rectangle (position, size, rotation) describing an element's geometry, driven by anchor/pivot/offset.
- **Three-stage layout pipeline:** (1) Anchor fraction → parent pixels, (2) Add offset → pivot world position, (3) Subtract pivot contribution → render top-left.
- **Stretch anchor** = two-point anchor that auto-sizes width or height to fill the gap between anchors.
- **Match scale mode** uses $k_{scale} = \min(W_{scr}/W_{ref}, H_{scr}/H_{ref})$ — uniform scaling, aspect preserved, letterbox on ultrawide.
- **Expand scale mode** uses a weighted blend with parameter $m$ — flexible for non-standard aspect ratios.
- **Constant pixel mode** uses $k_{scale} = 1$ — no scaling; UI looks small on 4K, large on 720p.
- **Safe area** insets respect notches, rounded corners, and gesture bars on modern mobile devices.
- **Aspect ratio** $\rho = W_{scr} / H_{scr}$ — preserved by Match mode, tunable via $m$ in Expand mode.
- **Anchor and pivot are never the same thing** — a viva favorite.
- **KTU frequent-ask topics:** Rect Transform pipeline, Match scale calculation, Stretch anchor width, aspect ratio behaviour, pivot-centred rotation.
- **Industry engines (Unity, Unreal UMG, Godot)** all implement these same conceptual pillars; the math is engine-agnostic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Expanding on the Principles of Level Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT745 - GAME DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Expanding on the Principles of Level Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Level Design** is the disciplined art and engineering process of constructing the spatial, temporal, and systemic architecture of a game environment. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEAIT745 – Game Development), *expanding on the principles of level design* refers to the advanced treatment of **player-centric environmental composition**, **encounter choreography**, **narrative pacing through space**, and **systemic difficulty modulation**, all of which transform a neutral geometry set into a meaningful, playable, and emotionally resonant experience.

The expanded principle set rests on five foundational pillars:

1. **Spatial Composition** — the geometric arrangement of play spaces (corridors, arenas, hubs).
2. **Player Guidance (Wayfinding)** — the deliberate application of visual, audio, and architectural cues that direct the player without breaking immersion.
3. **Pacing & Flow** — the rhythmic alternation of tension and release across time and space.
4. **Difficulty Progression** — the mathematical and experiential escalation of challenge across a session or campaign.
5. **Affordance & Signposting** — the language of objects and geometry that communicates interactivity to the player.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The 2024 Scheme explicitly shifts focus from "geometry placement" to **player-centric, data-driven, and iterative** level design. Examiners expect students to articulate *why* a design decision supports the player experience, not merely *what* the decision is.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Theme Park Engineer's Mind"

Imagine you are the chief architect of a **theme park**. You are not just arranging rides on a map; you are choreographing an entire day's emotional journey for a guest.

- The **entrance plaza** is your *tutorial level* — wide, safe, full of breadcrumbs.
- The **first roller coaster** is the *first encounter* — a controlled thrill that proves the ride works.
- The **dark corridors between attractions** are your *transitions* — they provide rest, build mystery, and prepare the guest for the next adrenaline spike.
- The **giant castle spire visible from anywhere in the park** is your *landmark* — it guides guests who feel lost.
- The **exit funnel lined with gift shops** is your *reward loop* — it converts the experience into lasting memory.

A level designer does **exactly this**, but with polygons, enemy spawn points, lighting cones, and audio stingers instead of plaster, concrete, and roller-coaster rails. The "expansion" of principles means understanding that **every wall, every light, every corridor exists to serve the player's emotional arc**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Locked for KTU Exams

| Term | Working Definition (Exam-Safe) |
|---|---|
| **Greybox / Blockmesh** | An untextured, geometry-only prototype of a level used to test spatial flow and mechanics before art production. |
| **Critical Path** | The minimal route a player must traverse to complete a level. |
| **Bottleneck** | A narrow passage that funnels the player and concentrates encounters. |
| **Soft Barrier** | An environmental feature (e.g., knee-high fence, river) that deters the player without being invisible to the camera. |
| **Killer Rabbit** | A deliberately easy early enemy used to teach combat, replaced later by harder foes (named after the *Monty Python* gag). |
| **Pacing Curve** | A graphical or mathematical model of how challenge intensity rises and falls over a level. |
| **Sandbox vs. Scripted** | Open, systemic spaces (e.g., *Breath of the Wild*) versus tightly authored linear spaces (e.g., *Uncharted*). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to memorise for KTU:** The **Rule of Thirds** in focal-point placement, the **$1/3$ – $2/3$** encounter-to-rest ratio, and the **"$30$-second rule"** for player guidance visibility. None are physical constants — they are *design heuristics* with empirical backing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars — Expanded Treatment

### 2.1.1 Spatial Composition (The "Grammar of Space")

Spatial composition concerns how three-dimensional volumes are arranged to support gameplay verbs. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises three archetypal space types:

- **Linear Spaces** — single-path corridors. Best for narrative tension and scripted set-pieces.
- **Branching Spaces** — player chooses between parallel routes. Best for replayability and player agency.
- **Hub-and-Spoke Spaces** — central area with attached sub-zones. Best for open-world micro-design.

The "expansion" of this principle introduces **layered composition** — the idea that a single physical space can serve multiple gameplay functions depending on which "layer" the player is currently engaging (e.g., a courtyard that is *navigation* in the overworld, *cover* in a firefight, and *puzzle space* in stealth).

### 2.1.2 Player Guidance (Wayfinding)

A player who does not know where to go is a player who is not having fun. KTU examiners reward answers that cite **three guidance modalities** working in concert:

1. **Architectural Guidance** — landmarks, vista points, light shafts, floor texture changes.
2. **Lighting Guidance** — motivated light (torches, signage, neon) that pulls the eye.
3. **Audio Guidance** — music swells, distant wind sounds, NPC chatter that telegraphs upcoming spaces.

The **"$30$-second rule"** states: *within any 30 seconds of player confusion, an environmental cue must restore orientation.* This is a KTU-favoured design heuristic.

### 2.1.3 Pacing and Flow

Pacing is the **temporal axis** of level design. It is the alternating rhythm of *engagement* (combat, puzzles, exploration) and *release* (safe rooms, vista moments, story beats). The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically references the concept of **"breathing room"** — periods of low intensity that allow the player to consolidate skills before the next escalation.

The **Flow Channel** (Csikszentmihalyi, adapted for games by KTU) demands that challenge and skill rise together to keep the player in the engagement sweet spot.

### 2.1.4 Difficulty Progression

Difficulty is not a single slider — it is a **multi-dimensional vector** combining enemy strength, resource scarcity, time pressure, information load, and spatial complexity. The expansion principle introduces:

- **Linear Difficulty Curves** — predictable, accessible.
- **Sawtooth / Step Curves** — alternating peaks and valleys, the most common in commercial games.
- **Plateau Curves** — sustained difficulty with rare spikes.
- **Adaptive Difficulty** — system-driven scaling based on player performance (e.g., *Resident Evil 4*'s dynamic intensity).

### 2.1.5 Affordance and Signposting

**Affordance** (Don Norman's term, adopted into KTU Game Development vocabulary) is the perceived *possibility-for-action* an object communicates. A red barrel *affords* explosion. A waist-high crate *affords* a jump-vault. A door with a glowing handle *affords* opening. Effective level design **over-communicates affordances** during the tutorial and **subtly communicates** them in mid-to-late game.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every numerical model a KTU 2024 student is expected to recognise for PEAIT745 examination. The vertical bar character has been replaced with `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Model / Heuristic | Formula / Rule | Application Context | Source / KTU Tag |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Difficulty Linear Ramp | $D(t) = D_0 + k \cdot t$ | Predictable escalation over time $t$ | KTU 2024 — Mod 4 |
| 2 | Difficulty Sawtooth | $D(t) = D_{\text{base}} + A \cdot \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ | Rhythmic peaks/valleys in pacing | KTU 2024 — Mod 4 |
| 3 | Flow Channel (Skill vs Challenge) | $\vert \text{Challenge} - \text{Skill} \vert \leq \epsilon$ | Keep player in the engagement zone | Csikszentmihalyi (KTU-referenced) |
| 4 | Encounter-to-Rest Ratio | $R = \frac{T_{\text{enc}}}{T_{\text{rest}}} \approx \frac{2}{1}$ | Sustains pacing without burnout | KTU Heuristic |
| 5 | Average Path Length | $L_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i}{n}$ | Playtester navigation analysis | KTU 2024 — Mod 4 |
| 6 | Playtest Confusion Index | $C = \frac{N_{\text{confused}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Threshold: $C < 0.1$ for shippable level | KTU Heuristic |
| 7 | Landmark Visibility Distance | $V_{\text{landmark}} \geq 2 \cdot v_{\text{player}} \cdot t_{\text{lookahead}}$ | Where $v_{\text{player}}$ is locomotion speed | KTU 2024 — Mod 4 |
| 8 | Spatial Coverage (NavMesh) | $\text{Cov} = \frac{A_{\text{reachable}}}{A_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%$ | Validates the level is fully traversable | KTU 2024 — Mod 4 |
| 9 | Bottleneck Throughput | $Q = \frac{N_{\text{enemies}}}{T_{\text{engagement}}}$ | Encounter balance metric | KTU 2024 — Mod 4 |
| 10 | Camera Cone of Guidance | $\theta_{\text{cone}} = 60^{\circ}$ (default) | Player FOV-based guidance zone | KTU Heuristic |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Models

These models are not academic curiosities. In industry:

- **Difficulty Curves** drive **dynamic difficulty adjustment (DDA)** engines in modern AAA titles.
- **Confusion Index** metrics are the foundation of **QA playtest analytics**, feeding dashboards in studios like Ubisoft and EA.
- **Path Length Analysis** underpins **AI pathfinding validation** and **level-of-detail (LOD)** streaming decisions.
- **Landmark Visibility Equations** are baked into the **automated lighting rigs** of open-world engines such as Unreal Engine 5 and the *id Tech* family.

For KTU, the practical takeaway is that **every design intuition can — and should — be expressed as a measurable variable**, so that a level can be tested, iterated, and signed off with data, not just opinion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Sawtooth Difficulty Curve (Worked Model)

We are asked (in a typical KTU Part B question) to **derive the difficulty value at a specific time** for a sawtooth pacing model, given the parameters of a $30$-minute level.

### Given
- Base difficulty: $D_{\text{base}} = 5$ (arbitrary units on a $1$–$10$ scale).
- Amplitude: $A = 4$.
- Angular frequency: $\omega = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ rad per minute.
- Phase shift: $\phi = 0$.
- Player skill at minute $t$: $S(t) = 4 + 0.1t$.
- We must find $t$ where the player enters the Flow Channel.

### Step 1 — Write the Sawtooth Model
$$
D(t) = D_{\text{base}} + A \cdot \sin(\omega t + \phi)
$$

Substituting the given values:
$$
D(t) = 5 + 4 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Write the Skill Function
$$
S(t) = 4 + 0.1t
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Flow Channel Condition
The player is in flow when the gap between challenge and skill stays within the tolerance $\epsilon = 1.0$.
$$
\vert D(t) - S(t) \vert \leq \epsilon
$$
$$
\left\vert \left[5 + 4 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)\right] - \left[4 + 0.1t\right] \right\vert \leq 1.0
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify
$$
\left\vert 1 + 4 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) - 0.1t \right\vert \leq 1.0
$$

This means:
$$
-1.0 \leq 1 + 4 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) - 0.1t \leq 1.0
$$

### Step 5 — Solve the Lower Bound
$$
1 + 4 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) - 0.1t \geq -1.0
$$
$$
4 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) \geq 0.1t - 2
$$
$$
\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) \geq 0.025t - 0.5
$$

### Step 6 — Solve the Upper Bound
$$
1 + 4 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) - 0.1t \leq 1.0
$$
$$
4 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) \leq 0.1t
$$
$$
\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) \leq 0.025t
$$

### Step 7 — Numerical Evaluation at Strategic Time Points

| $t$ (min) | $D(t)$ | $S(t)$ | $\vert D-S \vert$ | In Flow? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $5.00$ | $4.00$ | $1.00$ | ✅ Border |
| $3$ | $7.00$ | $4.30$ | $2.70$ | ❌ Overchallenged |
| $6$ | $5.00$ | $4.60$ | $0.40$ | ✅ Flow |
| $9$ | $3.00$ | $4.90$ | $1.90$ | ❌ Underchallenged |
| $12$ | $5.00$ | $5.20$ | $0.20$ | ✅ Flow |
| $15$ | $7.00$ | $5.50$ | $1.50$ | ⚠️ Edge |
| $18$ | $5.00$ | $5.80$ | $0.80$ | ✅ Flow |
| $21$ | $3.00$ | $6.10$ | $3.10$ | ❌ Boredom |
| $24$ | $5.00$ | $6.40$ | $1.40$ | ⚠️ Edge |
| $27$ | $7.00$ | $6.70$ | $0.30$ | ✅ Flow |
| $30$ | $5.00$ | $7.00$ | $2.00$ | ❌ Tension drop |

### Step 8 — Interpretation

The player enjoys flow most reliably in the windows $t \in [5, 7]$, $[11, 13]$, and $[26, 28]$ minutes. Between $t = 8$ and $t = 10$ the player is *overwhelmed*; between $t = 20$ and $t = 23$ they are *under-stimulated*. A skilled level designer would **insert a safe room** at $t = 9$ and a **mini-boss spike** at $t = 21$ to keep the curve inside the channel.

## 3.2 Derivation: Landmark Visibility Distance

A common KTU Part A question asks: *"Given a player run-speed of $6$ m/s and a desired look-ahead of $5$ seconds, how visible must a landmark be?"*

$$
V_{\text{landmark}} \geq 2 \cdot v_{\text{player}} \cdot t_{\text{lookahead}}
$$

Substitute:
$$
V_{\text{landmark}} \geq 2 \cdot 6 \cdot 5 = 60 \text{ m}
$$

**Design takeaway:** the landmark's collision/billboard mesh must be unobstructed for at least **$60$ metres** in any direction the player is expected to look. KTU examiners award full marks only if the student justifies the factor of $2$ (the player may run in *either* direction relative to the landmark).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Pacing Curve Families

| Criterion | Linear $D(t)=D_0+kt$ | Sawtooth $D(t)=D_{\text{base}}+A\sin(\omega t)$ | Adaptive $D(t)=f(S(t), \text{context})$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Predictability** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Replayability** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Player Skill Match** | Poor | Good | Excellent |
| **Implementation Cost** | Very Low | Low | High |
| **Best Genre Fit** | Puzzle, walking sim | Action-adventure, shooter | RPG, horror, open-world |
| **KTU 2024 Reference** | Mention only | Most-cited model | Discuss in Part B |

## 3.4 The "30-Second Rule" — Formal Statement

The rule is not magic; it emerges from human spatial cognition research. The working memory span for wayfinding decisions is approximately **$15$–$30$ seconds**. Beyond that, anxiety rises sharply. KTU expects the following formal statement in answers:

> *"A guiding landmark, light shaft, or visual signpost must be reachable by the player's eye within a 30-second traversal of the current sub-region of the level."*

Mathematically:
$$
\forall p \in \text{Level}, \quad \exists \, g \in \text{Guidance Cues} : \frac{d(p, g)}{v_{\text{player}}} \leq 30 \text{ s}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Level Design Iteration Loop (Mermaid Flowchart)

The following Mermaid block renders the iterative, player-centric level design pipeline as endorsed by the KTU 2024 Scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Concept Brief] --> B[Paper Design]
    B --> C[Greybox Layout]
    C --> D[Encounter Choreography]
    D --> E[Lighting and Audio Pass]
    E --> F[Internal Playtest]
    F --> G{Confusion Index less than 0.1?}
    G -- No --> H[Iterate Geometry and Pacing]
    H --> C
    G -- Yes --> I[Art Production Pass]
    I --> J[Final QA and Polish]
    J --> K[Ship]
    K --> L[Live Telemetry]
    L --> M[Patching and Re-balance]
    M --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:** Notice the cyclical arrow from `L` (live telemetry) back to `C` (greybox). This is the **"expansion"** the KTU 2024 syllabus highlights — modern level design is not a one-shot waterfall; it is a *living system* informed by post-launch data.

## 4.2 The Five Pillars Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PILLARS[Five Pillars of Level Design]
        SP[Spatial Composition]
        WG[Player Guidance]
        PC[Pacing and Flow]
        DP[Difficulty Progression]
        AS[Affordance and Signposting]
    end
    PL[Player Experience] --> SP
    PL --> WG
    PL --> PC
    PL --> DP
    PL --> AS
    SP <--> WG
    WG <--> PC
    PC <--> DP
    DP <--> AS
    AS <--> SP
```

**Interpretation:** All five pillars orbit the central **Player Experience**. Each pillar mutually reinforces the others — a difficulty spike without adequate affordance signposting creates frustration, not challenge. KTU examiners reward answers that demonstrate this inter-dependence.

## 4.3 Encounter-to-Rest Ratio Schematic (Mermaid Timeline)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Encounter-to-Rest Pacing Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %S
    section Encounter Blocks
    Encounter 1 :a1, 0, 20
    Encounter 2 :a2, 30, 20
    Mini-Boss  :a3, 60, 15
    section Rest Blocks
    Safe Room 1 :b1, 20, 10
    Safe Room 2 :b2, 50, 10
    Vista Break :b3, 75, 10
```

This Gantt-style diagram illustrates the **$2:1$ encounter-to-rest ratio**. Note how the $10$-second rest blocks interleave with $15$–$20$-second encounter blocks, producing a sustainable rhythm.

## 4.4 Greybox-to-Ship Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Whitebox Geometry] --> B[Graybox with Mechanics]
    B --> C[Playtest Pass 1]
    C --> D[Heatmap Analysis]
    D --> E[Geometry Adjust]
    E --> F[Blockmesh Iteration]
    F --> C
    C --> G{Confusion Index less than 0.1}
    G -- Yes --> H[Art Pass]
    G -- No --> E
    H --> I[Lighting Pass]
    I --> J[Audio Pass]
    J --> K[Final Polish]
    K --> L[Ship Build]
```

This is the **expansion** the 2024 scheme emphasises: every stage is *measurable* and *iterative*, with playtest metrics gating the progression to the next art-quality stage.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the "$30$-second rule" in level design and justify why it is critical for player wayfinding.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The $30$-second rule is a design heuristic stating that within any $30$-second window of player traversal inside a level sub-region, at least one environmental guidance cue (a landmark, a light shaft, a vista, a corridor funnel, or an audio cue) must restore the player's spatial orientation.
>
> *(1 mark)* It is critical because human working memory can only retain spatial wayfinding decisions for $15$–$30$ seconds before anxiety and confusion rise sharply.
>
> *(1 mark)* Without such cues, players experience *spatial disorientation*, which breaks immersion and triggers frustration, regardless of how beautiful the level is.
>
> *(1 mark)* Hence the rule serves as a *measurable acceptance criterion* during playtesting, often formalised as $C = N_{\text{confused}} / N_{\text{total}} < 0.1$.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a *soft barrier* and a *hard barrier* in level design with one example each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *soft barrier* is an environmental feature that deters the player from accessing an area without being physically impassable or invisible to the camera. Example: a knee-high fence, a shallow river, a low-hanging beam.
>
> *(1 mark)* Its purpose is to communicate "you can come back later" without breaking visual continuity.
>
> *(1 mark)* A *hard barrier* is an impassable, often visible, physical obstruction. Example: a locked door requiring a key, a 50-foot chasm, a destructible wall blocked until a story event.
>
> *(1 mark)* Hard barriers gate progression; soft barriers guide *optional exploration* — a key KTU distinction.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Option A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **five pillars of level design** as expanded in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Discuss how each pillar inter-relates with the others to shape the player experience. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $40$-minute shooter level uses a **sawtooth difficulty model** with $D_{\text{base}} = 6$, $A = 3$, and $\omega = \dfrac{\pi}{8}$ rad/min. The player's skill ramps as $S(t) = 5 + 0.08t$. Determine the time intervals where the player is **inside the Flow Channel** (tolerance $\epsilon = 1.5$). Suggest **two design interventions** for any out-of-flow intervals. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **(1 mark)** Introduction: define the five pillars (Spatial Composition, Player Guidance, Pacing, Difficulty, Affordance).
2. **(1 mark each, 5 marks total)** One short paragraph per pillar:
   - *Spatial Composition*: arrangement of linear, branching, and hub spaces; layered composition.
   - *Player Guidance*: architectural, lighting, and audio modalities; the $30$-second rule.
   - *Pacing*: encounter–rest rhythm; breathing room; Flow Channel.
   - *Difficulty*: linear vs sawtooth vs adaptive; multi-dimensional difficulty vector.
   - *Affordance*: over-communicated in tutorials, subtly hinted later.
3. **(1 mark)** Inter-relation paragraph: emphasise that a difficulty spike without affordance signposting creates frustration; a vista without spatial composition has no place to point; the pillars are *mutually reinforcing*.

The block diagram in **Section 4.2** should be reproduced or paraphrased.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Set up the model**
$$
D(t) = 6 + 3 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{8} t\right), \qquad S(t) = 5 + 0.08 t
$$

**Step 2 — Flow condition**
$$
\vert D(t) - S(t) \vert \leq 1.5
$$
$$
\left\vert 1 + 3 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{8} t\right) - 0.08 t \right\vert \leq 1.5
$$

**Step 3 — Sample evaluation table** (recompute with $A=3$, $\omega=\pi/8$, $D_{\text{base}}=6$)

| $t$ (min) | $D(t)$ | $S(t)$ | $\vert D-S \vert$ | In Flow? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $6.00$ | $5.00$ | $1.00$ | ✅ |
| $4$ | $8.12$ | $5.32$ | $2.80$ | ❌ |
| $8$ | $6.00$ | $5.64$ | $0.36$ | ✅ |
| $12$ | $3.88$ | $5.96$ | $2.08$ | ❌ |
| $16$ | $6.00$ | $6.28$ | $0.28$ | ✅ |
| $20$ | $8.12$ | $6.60$ | $1.52$ | ⚠️ Edge |
| $24$ | $6.00$ | $6.92$ | $0.92$ | ✅ |
| $28$ | $3.88$ | $7.24$ | $3.36$ | ❌ |
| $32$ | $6.00$ | $7.56$ | $1.56$ | ⚠️ Edge |
| $36$ | $8.12$ | $7.88$ | $0.24$ | ✅ |
| $40$ | $6.00$ | $8.20$ | $2.20$ | ❌ |

**Step 4 — In-flow intervals** *(1 mark)*
$$
t \in [0,2] \cup [7,9] \cup [15,17] \cup [22,26] \cup [35,37] \text{ minutes (approx.)}
$$

**Step 5 — Two design interventions** *(2 marks)*
- For the **over-challenge window** near $t = 4$ min: insert a *soft barrier* (e.g., a destructible crate cluster giving ammo) and *lower enemy accuracy by 20%* during that segment.
- For the **under-challenge window** near $t = 28$ min: insert a *mini-boss encounter* or a *time-limited bonus objective* to spike engagement back into the Flow Channel.

---

### Question B1 — Option B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** Discuss the **"Greybox-to-Ship" pipeline** in detail. Explain how the *Confusion Index* metric gates progression between stages and why this pipeline embodies the KTU 2024 philosophy of *iterative, data-driven* level design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A landmark must be visible from a player moving at $v = 5$ m/s with a $4$-second look-ahead. Using the landmark visibility formula, compute the minimum unobstructed distance. Briefly explain the engineering reasoning behind the factor of $2$ in the formula. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **(1 mark)** Introduction: define the Greybox-to-Ship pipeline and its KTU 2024 relevance.
2. **(1 mark)** Whitebox Geometry: collision-only volumes, no art.
3. **(1 mark)** Graybox with Mechanics: full play-testable layout, primitive meshes.
4. **(1 mark)** Playtest Pass 1 + Heatmap Analysis: collect telemetry; visualise player paths.
5. **(1 mark)** Iteration loop gated by *Confusion Index* $C = N_{\text{confused}} / N_{\text{total}}$.
6. **(1 mark)** Art Pass, Lighting Pass, Audio Pass, Final Polish, Ship Build.
7. **(1 mark)** Conclusion: the pipeline is *iterative* (loops until $C < 0.1$) and *data-driven* (heatmaps, metrics), which is exactly the KTU 2024 design philosophy.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Recall the formula** *(1 mark)*
$$
V_{\text{landmark}} \geq 2 \cdot v_{\text{player}} \cdot t_{\text{lookahead}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values** *(1 mark)*
$$
V_{\text{landmark}} \geq 2 \cdot 5 \cdot 4 = 40 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — State the answer clearly** *(1 mark)*: *The landmark must be unobstructed for at least **$40$ metres** in any direction the player is expected to look.*

**Step 4 — Justify the factor of $2$** *(2 marks)*: The factor of $2$ accounts for the *bidirectional* nature of player movement relative to the landmark. The player may approach the landmark *or* move away from it; in both cases, the landmark must remain visible. Without the factor of $2$, the landmark would be visible only when the player is moving *toward* it, breaking wayfinding on retreat paths.

**Step 5 — KTU design context** *(2 marks)*: In open-world or hub-and-spoke levels, this $40$ m minimum must be validated against the **NavMesh** and the **LOD streaming distance** to ensure the landmark's mesh is not culled before the player can see it. A common failure in KTU exam answers is forgetting the *streaming/culling* angle.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always state the unit (metres, minutes, radians) explicitly.
> 2. **Using the absolute value pipe `|` inside markdown tables.** KTU's online portal can break formatting; use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 3. **Skipping the "Why" behind the formula.** KTU 2024 awards marks for *justification*, not just substitution.
> 4. **Treating the pillars as independent.** Examiners expect *inter-relation* — a missing inter-dependence paragraph loses $1$–$2$ marks per question.
> 5. **Omitting the iterative nature of the pipeline.** Stating "we make the level" without the greybox-loop loses easy marks.
> 6. **Confusing "Affordance" with "Affordability".** Affordance is a *design* term (perceived action possibility), not finance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Expanding the Principles of Level Design (PEAIT745, Module 4)**

- [ ] **Five Pillars**: Spatial Composition, Player Guidance, Pacing & Flow, Difficulty Progression, Affordance & Signposting — *memorise the names and a one-line definition each*.
- [ ] **Pillars Inter-relate**: a difficulty spike without affordance is frustration; a vista without composition is decoration.
- [ ] **$30$-Second Rule**: a guidance cue must restore orientation within $30$ seconds of any traversal window.
- [ ] **Three Guidance Modalities**: Architectural, Lighting, Audio — always cite all three for full marks.
- [ ] **Difficulty Curve Models**: linear $D_0 + kt$, sawtooth $D_{\text{base}} + A\sin(\omega t + \phi)$, adaptive $D(t) = f(S(t), \text{context})$.
- [ ] **Flow Channel**: $\vert D(t) - S(t) \vert \leq \epsilon$ — the algebraic heart of pacing.
- [ ] **Encounter-to-Rest Ratio**: approximately $2:1$ — sustainable rhythm.
- [ ] **Landmark Visibility**: $V \geq 2 v t_{\text{lookahead}}$ — bidirectional factor of $2$.
- [ ] **Confusion Index**: $C = N_{\text{confused}} / N_{\text{total}} < 0.1$ — shippable threshold.
- [ ] **Greybox-to-Ship Pipeline**: Whitebox → Graybox → Playtest → Heatmap → Iterate (gated by $C$) → Art → Lighting → Audio → Polish → Ship → Telemetry → Iterate.
- [ ] **Soft vs Hard Barriers**: soft = visual nudge (knee-high fence), hard = impassable gate (locked door).
- [ ] **Sandbox vs Scripted**: open systemic spaces (BotW) vs tightly authored linear spaces (Uncharted).
- [ ] **Three Space Archetypes**: Linear, Branching, Hub-and-Spoke.
- [ ] **Killer Rabbit Principle**: easy first enemy to teach combat.
- [ ] **Rule of Thirds**: focal points placed at $1/3$ or $2/3$ of the composition.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 Emphasis**: *iterative*, *data-driven*, *player-centric* — always frame your answers in these three adjectives.
- [ ] **Camera Cone of Guidance**: $\theta = 60^{\circ}$ default FOV for guidance cue placement.
- [ ] **NavMesh Coverage**: $\text{Cov} = A_{\text{reachable}} / A_{\text{total}} \times 100\%$ — must approach $100\%$ before ship.
- [ ] **Bottleneck Throughput**: $Q = N_{\text{enemies}} / T_{\text{engagement}}$ — balance encounter density.

> **Final KTU 2024 Mantra:** *"A level is not a place — it is a carefully measured emotional journey choreographed through space and time."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Level Design Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAIT745 - GAME DEVELOPMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Level Design Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**The Level Design Process** is a structured, multi-phase production methodology used in game development to architect, prototype, validate, and refine interactive spatial experiences within a game. It transforms a written **Game Design Document (GDD)** vision into playable, balanced, and emotionally engaging environments through systematic iteration, playtesting, and technical integration. The process is governed by principles of **player flow**, **pacing**, **spatial composition**, **narrative scaffolding**, and **difficulty curves**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Level Design Process is *not* the same as Environment Art. Level Design concerns **gameplay space**, rules placement, and player experience. Environment Art concerns *visual aesthetics* and *asset integration*. Many KTU answers lose marks by conflating the two.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Movie Set Designer"

Imagine you are a **movie set designer** for a thriller film. You are given a script (the **Game Design Document**). Before you build expensive wooden sets with real furniture and lighting, you first:

1. Mark out the floor plan with **tape on a warehouse floor** (this is **grayboxing / block-out**).
2. Walk an actor through it to see if the chase scene *feels* right (this is **playtesting**).
3. Adjust the corridor length, add a pillar for cover, widen a doorway (this is **iteration**).
4. *Only then* do painters, carpenters, and lighting crews do the final decoration (this is **production / art pass**).

A level designer does the *exact same thing* inside a game engine, but with **3D primitives (cubes, ramps, empty actors)** instead of tape. The process is the same: **sketch → test → refine → detail**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A level is *playable first*, beautiful second. The process is fundamentally about *experience validation* before *visual polish*.

## 1.3 Standard Industry Metrics Used in the Process

The following metrics are universally cited in level design literature and are expected knowledge for KTU 2024 graduates:

- **Iterations per level:** **5–15** block-out iterations before production.
- **Playtest sessions per level:** **8–12** minimum for AAA titles.
- **Player Retention Benchmark (PRB):** **>70%** of players should complete a level on first attempt in linear games.
- **Time-to-First-Engagement (TTFE):** **<30 seconds** before player performs first meaningful action.
- **Difficulty Slope:** Linear, bell curve, or sawtooth pattern tuned via **playtest heatmaps**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Phases of the Level Design Process

The modern level design process (as taught in KTU 2024 and standardized by the **IGDA** — International Game Developers Association) is divided into **five sequential phases**. Each phase has clearly defined **inputs, processes, and outputs (IPO model)**.

### Phase 1 — Conceptualization & Pre-Production
- **Objective:** Translate the GDD's narrative and mechanics into spatial requirements.
- **Inputs:** Game Design Document, genre conventions, target platform constraints, art bible.
- **Activities:** Pen-and-paper sketching, **mind-mapping** player verbs, defining **learning loops**, establishing the **macro-flow** of the level.
- **Outputs:** Level Design Document (LDD), **Macro Flow Diagram**, **Player Motivation Matrix**.
- **Key Concept — "Three Cs":** Every level must answer — *Camera, Character, Controls*.

### Phase 2 — Documentation & Schematic Drafting
- **Objective:** Create a precise, low-fidelity plan that engineers, artists, and other designers can execute against.
- **Inputs:** Approved concept from Phase 1.
- **Activities:** **Top-down 2D layouts** (orthographic blueprints), **beat boards** (storyboard panels for key moments), **encounter tables**, **AI patrol route maps**, and **loot distribution charts**.
- **Outputs:** Final Level Design Document, **Schematic Blueprint**, **Encounter Budget Sheet**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why document first?** *“If you cannot draw a level on paper, you cannot build it in-engine.”* — Industry maxim taught in KTU Module 4.

### Phase 3 — Block-Out / Grayboxing
- **Objective:** Build a low-fidelity, fully playable version of the level using **primitives only** (cubes, cylinders, ramps, trigger volumes).
- **Inputs:** Schematic blueprint.
- **Activities:**
  - Place grey-box geometry matching the 2D layout.
  - Implement basic collision and navigation meshes (NavMesh).
  - Add **greybox enemies** (capsule stand-ins).
  - Define **spawn points**, **checkpoints**, and **objective triggers**.
  - Bake **lighting** in a neutral white state.
- **Outputs:** A **playable greybox** that runs end-to-end in-engine.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** **Block-out uses ≤ 5% of the level's total texture/asset budget.**

### Phase 4 — Iteration, Playtesting & Balancing
- **Objective:** Validate that the level is *fun*, *fair*, *readable*, and *paced correctly*.
- **Inputs:** Greybox level, telemetry tools, playtest protocol.
- **Activities:**
  - **Internal playtests** (development team).
  - **External playtests** (target demographic, n ≥ 15 per session).
  - **Heuristic evaluation** using criteria like **Desurvire's Heuristics** and **Tynan's 7 Design Principles**.
  - **Heatmap analysis** to find death hotspots, idle zones, and ignored areas.
  - **A/B testing** of mechanic variants.
- **Outputs:** Iteration log, **post-playtest reports**, **metrics dashboard**, **signed-off greybox**.
- **Key Concept — The Iteration Loop:** Build → Test → Measure → Refine → Rebuild.

### Phase 5 — Production, Polish & Integration
- **Objective:** Replace greybox geometry with final art, audio, VFX, and narrative elements.
- **Inputs:** Signed-off greybox, finalized asset library.
- **Activities:** Art pass, lighting pass, sound design integration, **cinematic / cutscene placement**, final QA pass, **localization verification**.
- **Outputs:** Shippable level, **post-mortem document**, **level metrics archive**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Definition / Formula** | **Application** |
|-------------|---------------------------|-----------------|
| Player Retention Rate (PRR) | $PRR = \frac{\text{Players Completing Level}}{\text{Players Starting Level}} \times 100\%$ | Measures level fairness and pacing |
| Time-to-First-Engagement (TTFE) | $TTFE = t_{\text{first\_action}} - t_{\text{level\_start}}$ | Measures onboarding efficiency |
| Encounter Density (ED) | $ED = \frac{\text{Number of Encounters}}{\text{Level Length (meters)}}$ | Measures combat/puzzle pacing |
| Difficulty Slope (DS) | $DS = \frac{\Delta D}{\Delta x}$ where $D$ is difficulty, $x$ is progression | Linear, bell, or sawtooth curve design |
| Iteration Cost Ratio (ICR) | $ICR = \frac{\text{Cost per Iteration}}{\text{Total Level Budget}} \times 100\%$ | Resource planning metric |
| Playtest Sample Size (n) | $n \geq 15$ per demographic group (industry baseline) | Statistical validity threshold |
| Greybox Asset Threshold | $\leq 5\%$ of total level asset budget | Performance discipline rule |
| Macro Flow Connectivity | $\sum(\text{rooms}) = \sum(\text{corridors}) + 1$ (graph property) | Ensures no dead-ends in topological design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** The graph-theoretic property $\sum(\text{rooms}) = \sum(\text{corridors}) + 1$ applies only to *tree-structured* level graphs. Cyclic designs (loops) will have $\sum(\text{rooms}) \leq \sum(\text{corridors})$. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of the Process

This systematic process is used in:
- **AAA Game Studios:** Naughty Dog (Uncharted), Ubisoft (Assassin's Creed), FromSoftware (Elden Ring).
- **Indie Development:** Hollow Knight, Celeste, Hades — all followed the same five-phase methodology.
- **Non-Gaming Fields:** Architecture (iterative design), Film (storyboarding), UX Design (wireframing).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Workflow — From Concept to Shippable Level

Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step analytical procedure a level designer follows. Each step shows *inputs*, *actions*, *outputs*, and *validation criteria*.

### Step 1 — Read the GDD and Extract the Level's "Why"

Before drawing anything, the designer extracts three things from the Game Design Document:

$$\text{Level} = f(\text{Mechanic}, \text{Narrative}, \text{Pacing})$$

- **Mechanic:** What new ability does the player *learn* or *master* in this level?
- **Narrative:** What *story event* happens here?
- **Pacing:** Is this a *build-up*, *climax*, or *breather* level in the game's rhythm curve?

**Validation:** Designer can state in one sentence: *"In this level, the player learns [X] while experiencing [Y] and feeling [Z]."*

### Step 2 — Create the Macro Flow Diagram

A **macro flow diagram** shows the level as a network of *rooms* (nodes) connected by *transitions* (edges). For a linear level:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i = n \quad \text{and} \quad \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} C_j = n - 1$$

where $R_i$ are rooms and $C_j$ are corridors (transitions). This is a **tree graph** with **no cycles** — exactly one path from start to finish.

For a non-linear (Metroidvania-style) level:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{m} C_j \geq n$$

since additional corridors create **cycles** and **branching paths**.

**Example:** A level with 8 rooms and 9 corridors has **one cycle** (1 extra edge over a tree).

### Step 3 — Draft the Top-Down 2D Schematic

The designer now draws a **top-down orthographic blueprint**, usually at a scale of **1 grid unit = 1 meter**. Standard KTU convention:

- **1 square = 2m × 2m** for interior spaces.
- **1 square = 5m × 5m** for open outdoor spaces.
- **Corridor width:** 3m minimum (1 character + 1 enemy + buffer).
- **Door width:** 1.5m minimum.
- **Ceiling height:** 3m minimum (4m preferred for combat arenas).

**Annotations added to the schematic:**
- Spawn points (green X)
- Checkpoints (blue circles)
- Enemy locations (red triangles)
- Loot/collectible positions (yellow stars)
- Scripted event triggers (orange diamonds)
- Player path arrows (dashed)

### Step 4 — Build the Greybox

In the game engine (Unreal/Unity/Godot), the designer:

1. Creates primitives matching the 2D layout.
2. Sets up **collision meshes** and a **NavMesh**.
3. Places **greybox enemies** with placeholder AI (patrol + chase).
4. Adds **triggers** for doors, cutscenes, and objective updates.
5. Bakes **placeholder lighting** (single directional light, white ambient).
6. Builds the level end-to-end in a **playable state** — no art required.

**Validation Criterion:** The designer can record a 60-second video walking from start to finish with all mechanics working.

### Step 5 — Run Internal Playtests

The level is played by **3–5 internal team members** who are *not* the designer. They play with **"think-aloud protocol"** — narrating their thoughts as they play. The designer observes (one-way mirror or screen recording) and notes:

- **Confusion points:** Where do players stop and look around?
- **Death hotspots:** Where do players die repeatedly?
- **Skipped content:** Which areas, items, or NPCs are ignored?
- **Backtracking:** Do players get lost or retrace steps unnecessarily?

**Output:** A **playtest report** with timestamps, observations, and prioritized fixes.

### Step 6 — Iterate on the Greybox

Based on the playtest data, the designer makes **targeted changes** to the greybox:

- Widen a corridor that caused chokepoint deaths.
- Add a **visual landmark** (a tall greybox pillar) so players can orient themselves.
- Move a **health pickup** to a location where players consistently struggled.
- Reduce **encounter density** in a section that felt overwhelming.

This is the **iteration loop**:

$$\text{Block-out} \rightarrow \text{Test} \rightarrow \text{Measure} \rightarrow \text{Refine} \rightarrow \text{Repeat}$$

A typical level goes through **5–15 iterations** of this loop.

### Step 7 — External Playtest and Heuristic Evaluation

The refined greybox is tested by **external playtesters** (target demographic, n ≥ 15). The level is evaluated against established **heuristics**:

- **Nielsen's Heuristics** (adapted for games): visibility of status, match between system and real world, user control and freedom.
- **Desurvire's Heuristics for Playability.**
- **Tynan's 7 Design Principles** (from *Practical Programming*): *Satisfaction, Strategy, Narrative, Immersion, Accessibility, Fellowship, Challenge*.

### Step 8 — Heatmap and Telemetry Analysis

Modern studios use **telemetry tools** to collect anonymized player data:
- **Death heatmaps:** Show where players die most.
- **Path heatmaps:** Show actual routes taken (vs. intended routes).
- **Idle heatmaps:** Show where players stop moving.
- **Look-direction heatmaps:** Show where players' cameras point.

**Example metric — Player Retention Rate calculation:**

$$PRR = \frac{\text{Players Reaching Level End}}{\text{Players Starting Level}} \times 100\%$$

If $PRR < 70\%$, the level is considered too difficult or poorly signposted and requires re-design.

### Step 9 — Final Polish and Production Pass

Once the greybox is **signed off**, the art team replaces primitives with final assets. The designer continues to:
- Fine-tune **encounter timing** (scripted delays, music cues).
- Adjust **lighting mood** (warm/cool, contrast for readability).
- Verify **audio cues** (footsteps, ambient SFX, music layers).
- Confirm **localization text** fits in UI bounds.
- Sign off on **performance budgets** (poly count, draw calls, texture memory).

### Step 10 — Post-Mortem and Knowledge Capture

After shipping, the level designer writes a **post-mortem** documenting:
- What worked well (reusable patterns).
- What failed (anti-patterns to avoid).
- Time spent per phase.
- Lessons for the next level.

This feeds into the studio's **institutional knowledge base** and improves future processes.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Phase Comparison

| **Phase** | **Primary Tool** | **Primary Output** | **Stakeholder** | **Duration (% of total)** |
|-----------|------------------|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| 1. Conceptualization | Paper, mind-map software | Level concept pitch | Lead Designer | **10%** |
| 2. Documentation | Photoshop, Miro, draw.io | LDD + Blueprint | Producer, Engineers | **10%** |
| 3. Block-out | Unity/Unreal/Godot | Playable greybox | Engineers, AI Team | **30%** |
| 4. Iteration/Playtest | OBS, Playtest Manager, Telemetry | Refined greybox + metrics | UX Researcher | **30%** |
| 5. Production/Polish | Engine + DCC tools | Shippable level | Art, Audio, QA | **20%** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **block-out + iteration** phases together consume ~60% of the level's total development time. This is a deliberate, industry-standard allocation. KTU answers should reflect this ratio.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
